1.1: Leviticus is the central book of the Torah and the most liturgical: it contains the instructions for the five primary offerings (burnt, grain, peace, sin, guilt), the ordination of the Aaronic priesthood, the purity laws, the Holiness Code (chs. 17-26), and the festival calendar. Its context is the completed tabernacle (Exod 40) — YHWH's glory has filled the structure, and now YHWH speaks from within it (Lev 1:1: YHWH called to Moses and spoke to him from the tent of meeting). The book addresses the central problem of how a holy God can dwell among a sinful people — the answer is a complex system of sacrifice, priesthood, purification, and consecration. Hebrews reads Leviticus as the most detailed typological preparation for Christ's high-priestly work.
1.2: The five major offerings introduced across chapters 1-7: (1) the burnt offering (olah, chs. 1, 6:8-13), (2) the grain offering (minḥah, chs. 2, 6:14-23), (3) the fellowship offering (shelamim, chs. 3, 7:11-36), (4) the sin offering (ḥatta't, chs. 4-5:13, 6:24-30), (5) the guilt offering (asham, chs. 5:14-6:7, 7:1-10). Chapters 1-3 introduce the voluntary offerings; chapters 4-5 introduce the obligatory reparatory offerings. Sacrificial systems were universal in the ancient Near East — Mesopotamian (Sumerian and Akkadian texts), Ugaritic, and Egyptian systems all involved animal sacrifice, grain offerings, and blood rituals. What distinguishes Israel's system is its monolatrous structure (one altar, one authorized priesthood, one deity), its emphasis on atonement theology, and its explicit grounding in covenant.
1.3: The olah (burnt offering) is the most common offering in the OT. Its defining characteristic is total consumption: unlike other offerings where portions belong to the priest or offerer, the entire animal is burned on the altar — hence the LXX rendering holokautoma (whole-burnt). The animal must be from the herd (baqar) — domesticated cattle — and must be a male (zakhar) without defect (tamim, perfect/complete). The presentation at the entrance to the tent of meeting (petaḥ ohel mo'ed) makes the offering a public covenant act, not a private transaction.
1.4: The offerer — not the priest — performs the semikah (laying of hands) and the slaughter. This is a crucial contextual point often missed: in Israel's system, the layperson killed the animal; the priest handled the sacred elements (blood, fire, altar). This distribution of roles differs from some ANE systems where priests controlled all aspects of sacrifice. The offerer's personal act of slaughter makes the sacrifice more intimate — he kills his own animal; the death is not bureaucratically managed. The laying of hands transfers identification: the animal represents the offerer before YHWH.
1.5: The blood-sprinkling (zaraq) is the priestly act par excellence: Aaron's sons throw the blood against all sides of the altar. The Mesopotamian parallels help clarify what blood-manipulation meant in the ancient world: blood was understood as the carrier of life, and its manipulation at the sacred space was the mechanism of connection between the human and divine realms. In Israel's system, the specific theology is provided by Lev 17:11 — blood makes atonement because life is in it. The north side of the altar is specified for certain offerings (v. 11), reflecting a consistent spatial organization of the sanctuary courts.
1.6: The skinning and dismemberment of the animal is performed by the offerer. The skin of burnt offerings belonged to the priest (Lev 7:8) — a practical priestly income even when the offerer received no portion of the flesh. The careful dismemberment (head, fat, internal organs, legs) reflects a systematic process ensuring complete consumption on the altar. Archaeological evidence from ancient Israelite sanctuaries (Arad, Tel Dan) shows altar structures consistent with this burning protocol.
1.7: The priests are responsible for the altar fire — they light it and maintain it. The fire on the altar was to burn continuously (Lev 6:12-13 will specify this explicitly). In the ancient world, sacred fires were maintained at major temples as signs of divine presence; Egypt's temples had perpetual lamps; Rome's Vestal virgins maintained the sacred fire. Israel's altar fire functions similarly but is grounded in the Sinai revelation rather than civic religion.
1.8: The careful arrangement of pieces (arak) on the altar is a priestly responsibility. The precision reflects the sanctuary's organization: nothing is thrown on haphazardly; the entire process is ordered and intentional. The head and fat are specifically mentioned — the head represents the whole; the fat (ḥelev) is reserved for YHWH (Lev 3:16-17). The systematic arrangement of dismembered animals on the altar was likely visible from outside the sanctuary court and would have communicated the seriousness and structure of Israel's worship.
1.9: The washing of internal organs and legs before burning is a purification step — removing dung and digestive contents. This practical measure also signals that even the most basic aspects of sacrifice are performed with care. The phrase reiaḥ niḥoaḥ laYHWH (pleasing aroma to YHWH) is the standard formula for accepted offerings and occurs 43 times in the Pentateuch. It is anthropomorphic language for divine acceptance — not that YHWH literally smells, but that the offering is received with pleasure. Paul applies this language to Christ's offering in Eph 5:2 and to the Philippians' gift in Phil 4:18.
1.10: The graduated accessibility of the burnt offering: bull (v. 3) → sheep or goat (v. 10) → bird (v. 14). This descending scale democratizes access to YHWH — the wealthy bring bulls; the middle class bring sheep or goats; the poor bring birds. The same graduated scale appears in the sin offering (ch. 4-5), the cleansing of skin diseases (Lev 14), and post-birth purification (Lev 12:8). Mary and Joseph bring two birds at Jesus's presentation (Luke 2:24), indicating their household's economic status.
1.11: The north side of the altar is specified for the flock offering — consistent with the spatial organization of the court. The north side was the designated slaughter area for certain offerings, probably to keep the blood flow away from the sanctuary entrance on the east side. The altar's geography organized Israel's sacrificial activity spatially, turning the court into a purposefully arranged liturgical space.
1.12: The same dismemberment procedure for flock animals as for cattle — consistent treatment regardless of the animal's size. The systematic approach emphasizes that the offering's value lies not in its monetary worth but in its proper presentation. A goat properly offered is as accepted as a bull properly offered.
1.13: The washing of entrails and legs applies to all burnt offerings. The formula reiaḥ niḥoaḥ laYHWH closes each offering type — the aroma language signals that the offering has achieved its intended purpose of pleasing YHWH. In ANE temple texts, gods were described as smelling sacrifices favorably; the Gilgamesh Epic (tablet XI) describes the gods gathering like flies around Utnapishtim's sacrifice after the flood. Israel uses the same language but locates it within a covenantal rather than polytheistic framework.
1.14: The bird offering: turtledove (tor) or young pigeon (ben-yonah). Birds were the minimum viable sacrifice — accessible to the very poor. Their smaller size explains the modified procedure that follows: no dismemberment, different blood disposal. The specification of two species reflects the practical availability of different birds in the Levant — both were common, nest near human habitation, and could be obtained without significant expense.
1.15: The priest performs all acts for bird offerings — unlike cattle and flock offerings where the offerer participates. This reflects the bird's smaller scale: there is no skin, and the blood must be drained at the altar side rather than thrown in quantity. The wringing of the neck (rather than throat-cutting) is the standard method for small birds. The blood drain at the side of the altar achieves the same theological purpose as the sprinkling in larger offerings.
1.16: The crop and feathers are removed and disposed of in the ash heap on the east side of the altar. The ash heap (deshen) was a designated disposal area for sacrificial refuse — it was clean enough to be near the sanctuary but separated from the altar itself. The east side facing the entrance — the direction from which worshippers approached — was where the ash was deposited, perhaps as a visible sign of ongoing sacrificial activity.
1.17: The bird is split at the wings without complete division — it is presented as a whole unit spread open on the altar. The partial splitting exposes the interior for complete burning while maintaining the bird's wholeness. The closing formula reiaḥ niḥoaḥ laYHWH confirms that the bird offering is equally accepted as the bull offering — economic inequality does not create liturgical inequality before YHWH.

16.1: Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement, Lev 23:27-28) became the most solemn day in the Jewish calendar — the only mandatory communal fast in the Torah. In Second Temple Judaism, it was the one day the high priest entered the most holy place. When the temple was destroyed in 70 CE, the entire Yom Kippur sacrificial system became inoperative — Hebrews' argument that Christ's once-for-all sacrifice supersedes the annual Yom Kippur ritual became immediately relevant to Jewish Christians watching the temple system collapse. The phrase 'once for all' (ephapax, Heb 9:12; 10:10) is Hebrews' direct response to Yom Kippur's annual repetition.
16.2: YHWH said to Moses: Tell Aaron your brother not to come at any time into the Holy Place inside the veil, before the mercy seat that is on the ark, so that he may not die — the death-threat prohibition establishes that the most holy place is inaccessible except under the precisely controlled conditions of Yom Kippur. The restriction is specifically Aaronic (not just priestly): only the high priest may enter, and only on one day per year. In Egyptian temple practice, the innermost sanctuary containing the divine image was similarly restricted; only the highest priests (or the Pharaoh in ritual persona) could approach; the similarity in spatial restriction highlights the theological difference — the Israelite prohibition aims at protecting the covenant community from divine judgment, not protecting a divine image from human view.
16.3: But in this way Aaron shall come into the Holy Place: with a bull from the herd for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering — the high priest enters with specific prescribed animals, not freely. The bull is for Aaron's own atonement (vv.6,11-14) before he can intercede for the congregation; the sequence personal atonement → communal atonement is structurally fundamental to Yom Kippur. No parallel Mesopotamian rite requires the officiating priest to first atone for himself before performing communal atonement; the Levitical insistence on the priest's own sinfulness is theologically distinctive.
16.4: He shall put on the holy linen coat and shall have the linen undergarments on his body, and he shall tie the linen sash around his waist, and wear the linen turban; these are the holy garments — Yom Kippur's vestments are strikingly simpler than the regular high-priestly garments (Exod 28: gold, blue, purple, scarlet, precious stones, bells). The linen-only set (without gold or jewels) is the same type worn by ordinary priests; the high priest's self-reduction to ordinary priestly garb for the day's atonement work signals humility and vulnerability before YHWH. Linen is also associated with the resurrection/purity in later theology; its whiteness became the dominant Yom Kippur visual symbol (cf. Isa 1:18).
16.5: And he shall take from the congregation of the people of Israel two male goats for a sin offering and one ram for a burnt offering — the two goats for the communal sin offering are the unique feature of Yom Kippur not found elsewhere in Leviticus or the ANE sacrificial corpus; both goats constitute a single sin offering (cf. v.5: 'two male goats for a sin offering'), but their respective fates divide: one dies as a chatat, one is sent alive to Azazel.
16.6: Aaron shall offer the bull as a sin offering for himself and shall make atonement for himself and for his house — the bull sin offering for Aaron and his household precedes the communal goat sin offering; the high priest's personal atonement is a prerequisite for his capacity to atone for others. This structural requirement — the priest must be atoned for before he can atone on behalf of others — is emphasized in the Yom Kippur rubric and later in Heb 7:27 as a limitation that the perfect High Priest transcends.
16.7: Then he shall take the two goats and set them before YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the two-goat presentation before YHWH at the sanctuary entrance establishes that both goats are simultaneously offered as a single complex offering; neither is simply a random animal from the flock but a paired sacrificial unit whose fate is determined by lot (v.8). The lot-casting gives the selection divine rather than human determination.
16.8: And Aaron shall cast lots over the two goats, one lot for YHWH and one lot for Azazel (goral echad la-YHWH vegoral echad la-Azazel) — the Azazel lot is one of the most debated cruxes in Leviticus. Three main interpretive streams: (1) Azazel as a place name (a rocky/remote place — related to ez goat + azal remove/disappear, 'the goat of removal'); (2) a personal/demonic name for a wilderness spirit — supported by 1 Enoch 8 and 10, which describe Azazel as a fallen angel; (3) a descriptor meaning 'complete sending-away.' The parallel structure la-YHWH/la-Azazel suggests Azazel is a personal entity parallel to YHWH, which has led most modern scholars to accept the demonic-entity interpretation — the goat is sent back to the demonic source of disorder in the wilderness, not sacrificed to it.
16.9: And Aaron shall present the goat on which the lot fell for YHWH and use it as a sin offering — the YHWH-lot goat follows the standard chatat procedure: slaughter, blood manipulation on the kapporet, tent of meeting, and outer altar. Its function is the ritual purification of the sanctuary from the accumulated impurities of the year.
16.10: But the goat on which the lot fell for Azazel shall be presented alive before YHWH to make atonement over it, that it may be sent away into the wilderness to Azazel — the Azazel-goat is not slaughtered; it is presented alive before YHWH for the confession-and-laying-on-of-hands rite (vv.21-22) and then sent alive into the wilderness. The pairing of a slaughtered goat (sanctuary purification) and a released goat (sin-removal from the community) creates a comprehensive atonement that addresses both the sanctuary's defilement and the people's accumulated guilt.
16.11: Aaron shall present the bull as a sin offering for himself, and shall make atonement for himself and for his house. He shall kill the bull as a sin offering for himself — the second mention of the bull sin offering for Aaron; the repetition reinforces the structural priority of the high priest's own atonement. The bull blood will be brought inside the veil (v.14) in the same procedure as the goat blood (v.15), providing full atonement coverage for both priest and congregation.
16.12: And he shall take a censer full of coals of fire from the altar before YHWH, and two handfuls of sweet incense beaten small, and he shall bring it inside the veil — the incense screen is the high priest's protection in the most holy place: the incense cloud covers the kapporet (mercy seat) so that Aaron does not see it directly and die (v.13). In Mesopotamian ritual, priests carried incense as part of divine approach protocols — the incense as divine encounter protection is a shared ANE ritual element, but the Levitical specification that it protects the officiating priest from the divine presence above the ark is distinctive.
16.13: And he shall put the incense on the fire before YHWH, that the cloud of the incense may cover the mercy seat that is over the testimony, so that he does not die — the kapporet (mercy seat, or atonement-cover) is the golden lid of the ark of the covenant, flanked by the cherubim (Exod 25:17-22). YHWH speaks from between the cherubim (Exod 25:22); the incense cloud interposes between the high priest and this most direct divine manifestation point. The protective incense protocol implies that even the high priest, performing the most sacred act of the liturgical year, approaches YHWH under a kind of protective mediation.
16.14: And he shall take some of the blood of the bull and sprinkle it with his finger on the front of the mercy seat on the east side, and in front of the mercy seat he shall sprinkle the blood with his finger seven times — the blood manipulation on the kapporet proceeds in two stages: one application on the front east face, then seven sprinklings in front of the kapporet (downward, toward the floor or the front of the ark). The number seven (completeness) recurs throughout Levitical blood rituals; here it covers the full spatial surface of the most holy place with atoning blood.
16.15: Then he shall kill the goat of the sin offering that is for the people and bring its blood inside the veil and do with its blood as he did with the blood of the bull, sprinkling it over the mercy seat and in front of the mercy seat — the people's sin offering goat receives identical blood manipulation to the priest's bull; the spatial procedure in the most holy place is the same for both, ensuring comprehensive atonement coverage for priest and people alike. The double entry into the most holy place (for bull blood, then for goat blood) required two separate entries through the veil.
16.16: Thus he shall make atonement for the Holy Place, because of the uncleannesses of the people of Israel and because of their transgressions, all their sins — the explicit statement that the sanctuary itself requires atonement: the accumulated impurities and sins of the people throughout the year have penetrated and defiled even the most holy place. This understanding that sin defiles the sanctuary (rather than only the sinner) is a distinctive Levitical concept; the sanctuary must be periodically purified of this accumulated contamination, not merely the individual worshipers.
16.17: No one may be in the tent of meeting from the time he enters to make atonement in the Holy Place until he comes out and has made atonement for himself and for his house and for all the assembly of Israel — the total exclusion of all other persons from the tent of meeting during the high priest's entry into the most holy place creates the maximum possible sacral isolation. The high priest stands utterly alone before YHWH during the most critical moment of the liturgical year.
16.18: Then he shall go out to the altar that is before YHWH and make atonement for it, and shall take some of the blood of the bull and some of the blood of the goat, and put it on the horns of the altar all around — after the inner sanctuary's atonement, the outer altar receives blood from both the bull and the goat. The mixed blood (from both the priestly and communal offerings) applied to the outer altar ensures that this most publicly visible locus of worship is also covered by the day's atonement.
16.19: And he shall sprinkle some of the blood on it with his finger seven times, and cleanse it and consecrate it from the uncleannesses of the people of Israel — seven sprinklings on the outer altar (as on the kapporet) complete the purification cycle; ve-qiddesho (consecrate it, reconsecrate it) indicates that the altar's holiness has been compromised by the year's accumulated defilements and is now restored. The Yom Kippur procedure thus resets the entire sanctuary complex to its original consecrated state.
16.20: And when he has made an end of atoning for the Holy Place and the tent of meeting and the altar, he shall present the live goat — the sequence is complete: most holy place → tent of meeting → outer altar → now the live Azazel goat. The spatial movement from innermost to outermost mirrors the concentric holiness zones of the tabernacle; atonement proceeds from the most sacred center outward.
16.21: And Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat and confess over it all the iniquities of the people of Israel, and all their transgressions, all their sins. And he shall put them on the head of the goat and send it away into the wilderness by the hand of a man who is in readiness — the laying of both hands (shtei yadav) on the scapegoat's head is unique in Levitical ritual (other laying-on-of-hand rites use one hand, Lev 1:4); the full weight of priestly identification transfers the entire community's accumulated sin-load to the goat. The verbal confession is the only instance in the sacrificial legislation where the content of the atonement act is explicitly verbal; the comprehensive three-term sin-vocabulary (avonot, pesha'im, chata'ot) covers the full range of Israelite moral failure.
16.22: The goat shall bear all their iniquities on itself to a remote area, and he shall let the goat go free in the wilderness — the goat literally carries (nasa) the sins; the verb is the same used for prophetic sin-bearing (Isa 53:4,11,12). The destination is eretz gezeirah (a cut-off land, a remote/separated region), Azazel's domain. Second Temple practice added a rocky precipice (according to Mishnah Yoma 6:6) over which the goat was pushed to prevent its return; the Talmud mentions that a red thread tied to the precipice would turn white as a sign of divine acceptance, echoing Isa 1:18.
16.23: Then Aaron shall come into the tent of meeting and shall take off the linen garments that he put on when he went into the Holy Place and shall leave them there — the linen Yom Kippur vestments are left in the tent of meeting after the inner ceremonies; they are not taken out or reused. The spatial containment of the most-holy garments within the tent reflects the principle that what has been in the most holy place remains contaminated with its holiness and cannot return to ordinary use.
16.24: And he shall bathe his body in water in a holy place and put on his garments and come out and offer his burnt offering and the burnt offering of the people and make atonement for himself and for the people — the post-inner-sanctuary procedure: bathing (transition purification from the most holy contact), change to regular high-priestly garments, then burnt offerings. The personal burnt offering reaffirms Aaron's complete consecration after the day's most intimate divine encounter.
16.25: And the fat of the sin offering he shall burn on the altar — the fat portions of both sin offering animals (bull and goat) burn on the outer altar as the standard YHWH-portion; even on Yom Kippur the basic sacrificial mechanics remain consistent with the sacrifice laws of Lev 3–7.
16.26: And he who lets the goat go to Azazel shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in water, and afterward he may come into the camp — the person who led the Azazel goat to the wilderness contracts impurity from the contact with the sin-laden goat; the same laundering-and-bathing purification required after other impurity contacts (Lev 11:25,28,40; 14:8; 15:5-8) applies here. The impurity transmission confirms that the goat genuinely carried sin, not merely symbolically represented it.
16.27: The bull for the sin offering and the goat for the sin offering, whose blood was brought in to make atonement in the Holy Place, shall be carried outside the camp. Their skin and their flesh and their dung shall be burned up with fire — the two sin offering animals whose blood entered the most holy place are burned outside the camp, as with all inward-blood-manipulation sin offerings (Lev 4:12,21; 6:30). The outside-camp burning of the most-holy sin offering animals creates a spatial parallel to the outside-camp destination of the Azazel goat; both forms of sin removal go outside.
16.28: And he who burns them shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in water, and afterward he may come into the camp — the person who burns the sin offering carcasses also contracts impurity requiring laundering and bathing. The systematic impurity from contact with sin-laden sacrificial materials reflects the objective weight of the sin these offerings have borne.
16.29: And it shall be a statute to you forever that in the seventh month, on the tenth day of the month, you shall afflict yourselves (ve-initem et-nafshoteichem) and shall do no work, either the native or the stranger who sojourns among you — Yom Kippur's two community requirements: self-affliction (innui nefesh, which rabbinic tradition interprets as fasting plus five other abstentions: bathing, anointing, leather shoes, marital relations) and complete work-prohibition. The tenth of the seventh month (Tishri) positions Yom Kippur five days before Sukkoth and ten days after Rosh Hashanah (Feast of Trumpets), creating the 'ten days of awe' structure of the high holy day season in Jewish liturgical calendar.
16.30: For on this day shall atonement be made for you to cleanse you. You shall be clean before YHWH from all your sins — the definitive statement of Yom Kippur's scope: mikal chatoteichem lifney YHWH titharu (from all your sins before YHWH you shall be clean). The comprehensive cleansing covers the full year's accumulated sins of the entire community; the day's procedures together accomplish what no individual sacrifice could: a complete annual reset of the covenant relationship.
16.31: It is a Sabbath of solemn rest to you (shabbat shabbaton hi lachem), and you shall afflict yourselves; it is a statute forever — the double Sabbath formula shabbat shabbaton (the most intense Sabbath-rest) is used only for Yom Kippur (here and Lev 23:32) and for the Sabbath itself (Exod 31:15; 35:2; Lev 23:3), marking Yom Kippur as the highest-intensity day of rest in the liturgical calendar.
16.32: And the priest who is anointed and consecrated to serve as priest in his father's place shall make atonement, wearing the holy linen garments — the provision for successor high priests ensures the Yom Kippur procedure is not dependent on Aaron personally but perpetuates through the Aaronic line; whoever holds the high-priestly office wears the linen garments and performs the prescribed rites. The phrase tachat aviv (in his father's place) establishes the hereditary succession principle for the high priesthood.
16.33: He shall make atonement for the holy sanctuary, and he shall make atonement for the tent of meeting and for the altar, and he shall make atonement for the priests and for all the people of the assembly — the four-part atonement summary: sanctuary, tent of meeting, altar, people. The concentric spatial sequence (from innermost sacred to the community) recapitulates the procedural order of vv.14-19. The comprehensive scope — all sacred spaces and all people — confirms that Yom Kippur is a total-reset mechanism for the entire covenantal system.
16.34: And this shall be a statute forever for you, that atonement may be made for the people of Israel once in the year because of all their sins. And Moses did as YHWH commanded him — the closing statute-formula (chuqat olam) establishes Yom Kippur as a permanent annual institution; the once-per-year restriction (achat bashanah) is as theologically significant as the comprehensiveness of the atonement. The annual cycle of accumulated sin + annual atonement creates the rhythm of Israelite covenantal life until the once-for-all atonement of Christ (Heb 9:7,12,25-28).

2.1: The grain offering (minḥah) originally meant any tribute-gift (Gen 32:13-21; 2 Sam 8:2) before becoming a technical term for the grain sacrifice. In non-cultic contexts minḥah describes a gift presented to a superior — which captures something important about the offering: it is tribute, not primarily atonement. The fine flour (solet) mixed with oil and frankincense is the basic form. Fine flour was the most refined and expensive grade of grain — not rough-ground meal. Grain offerings were accessible to those who could not afford animals, though in practice they often accompanied animal offerings.
2.2: The priest takes a qomets (handful — the fist-load that could be scooped) as the azkarah (memorial/representative portion) and burns it. The remainder belongs to Aaron and his sons. This division — a token portion to YHWH, the rest to the priests — is the opposite of the burnt offering (entirely to YHWH). The grain offering served a practical function: it provided flour, oil, and frankincense for priestly sustenance. The word azkarah (memorial portion) reflects the idea that the burning portion causes YHWH to 'remember' (zakar) the offerer — a relational concept, not merely a mechanical ritual.
2.3: The grain offering is 'most holy' (qodesh qodashim) — the same designation as the holy of holies and as the sin and guilt offerings. This elevated status means it may only be eaten by the priests in the sacred precincts, not taken home. The grain offering thus functions as both tribute to YHWH and priestly provision — the sanctuary economy provided for its servants through the offerings of the community.
2.4: The oven-baked form of grain offering: unleavened loaves or wafers. The prohibition on leaven (ḥamets) for grain offerings connects to the Passover/exodus context (unleavened bread as the bread of affliction and haste) and to the principle that fermentation — a process of corruption and transformation — is inappropriate for what is offered to YHWH. The altar receives nothing that has undergone the fermenting process. This is not a hygiene rule but a purity-theology rule about what approaches the divine.
2.5: The griddle-prepared grain offering: fine flour unleavened, mixed with oil. The variety of preparation methods (oven, griddle, deep pan) reflects different contexts of preparation — some households had ovens; others used flat griddles on open fire. The grain offering law accommodates different cooking equipment, democratizing the offering across different economic and practical circumstances.
2.6: The griddle offering is broken into pieces and oil is poured on it before presentation. The piece-breaking parallels the dismemberment of animal offerings — both are presented in portions to the altar. The consistent oil-mixing creates a unified preparation across all grain offering forms: oil binds the offering and was itself a valuable commodity representing blessing and prosperity.
2.7: The deep-pan (or pot) preparation: fine flour with oil. This third method covers all standard cooking vessels of the ancient Israelite kitchen — oven, griddle, and pot. The law's comprehensiveness in covering all three ensures that no available preparation method is excluded from legitimate offering.
2.8: The transition from home to sanctuary: the prepared grain offering is brought to the priest, who carries it to the altar. The movement from domestic space (preparation) to sacred space (presentation) is the physical enactment of the offering's logic — it begins in the offerer's household and is completed in YHWH's household.
2.9: The azkarah (memorial portion) is burned; the rest belongs to Aaron and sons. The word azkarah appears 7 times in Leviticus — always for the burned representative portion of a grain offering. It functions as a token of the whole: the part offered to YHWH represents the whole offering and the whole person of the offerer. The reiaḥ niḥoaḥ formula signals acceptance.
2.10: The priestly remainder of the grain offering must be eaten without leaven (matsot) in the court of the tabernacle — most holy space for a most holy offering. The restriction to the sacred precincts mirrors the restriction on who may eat it (priests only, not their families in most cases). The priestly meal of grain offering remnants is itself a holy act, not merely priestly sustenance.
2.11: Two specific prohibitions: leaven and honey. Honey (devash) includes both bee honey and fruit syrup (date honey, grape syrup) — all fermentable sweet substances. Like leaven, honey initiates fermentation and is therefore excluded from the altar. Notably, the prohibition on burning honey or leaven on the altar does not prevent them from being offered as firstfruits (v. 12) — they can be brought to the sanctuary but not burned as altar offerings. This distinction shows that the prohibition is specifically about what is burned for YHWH, not what enters the sanctuary precincts at all.
2.12: Firstfruits (bikurim) may be brought to YHWH as an offering but not burned on the altar as a pleasing aroma. The firstfruits were the first portion of each harvest crop — their offering acknowledged YHWH's ownership of the land and its produce. The Feast of Firstfruits (Lev 23:9-14) and the Feast of Weeks/Shavuot (Lev 23:15-21) were organized around firstfruits offerings. Paul applies firstfruits language to Christ's resurrection (1 Cor 15:23) and to believers as the Spirit's firstfruits (Rom 8:23).
2.13: Salt of the covenant: every grain offering must be seasoned with salt; salt must never be omitted. Salt preserves food — it is the anti-fermenting agent, the opposite of leaven. As a preservative, it represents permanence and faithfulness: a salt covenant (brit melaḥ) is an irrevocable, permanent covenant (Num 18:19; 2 Chr 13:5). The command to season every offering with salt connects the individual sacrificial act to the permanent covenant relationship between YHWH and Israel. The altar, as the place of the covenant, receives salt with every offering.
2.14: Firstfruits grain offerings take a specific form: roasted grain (qali) or crushed kernels (geres). The roasted grain offering is the most primitive form — direct parching of grain heads over fire, accessible even before the harvest is fully processed. The grain offering timeline runs from the earliest harvested grain (roasted heads) to fully processed fine flour (ground and sieved). The offering system accommodates the entire harvest cycle.
2.15: The firstfruits grain offering follows the standard procedure: oil and frankincense are applied. The frankincense (levonah) was imported from southern Arabia (modern Oman/Yemen) and was expensive — its presence in grain offerings elevated their status. The frankincense was entirely burned as part of the azkarah, creating the pleasing aroma that marked acceptance.
2.16: The priest burns the azkarah from the crushed grain, oil, and all the frankincense — the entire frankincense portion goes to YHWH, even though the grain is only partially burned. This proportional treatment ensures the most aromatic and valuable element (frankincense) belongs entirely to YHWH. The reiaḥ niḥoaḥ formula closes the grain offering laws.

3.1: The fellowship offering (shelamim, also rendered 'peace offering' or 'well-being offering') is the only offering in which the offerer participates in the meal alongside the priest. This communal dimension distinguishes it from the burnt offering (entirely to YHWH) and makes it the closest OT analogue to a covenant meal. The shelamim is derived from shalom (completeness, peace, well-being) — it is an offering of a person who is at peace with YHWH and wishes to celebrate or maintain that relationship. Male or female animals from the herd are acceptable (contrast the burnt offering's male-only requirement).
3.2: The offerer performs the slaughter at the sanctuary entrance — same as the burnt offering. The fellowship offering was often a celebratory sacrifice: celebrating answered prayer, completing a vow, or expressing spontaneous gratitude. Its communal nature (offerer eats a portion) made it the occasion for communal feasts. 1 Samuel 1:3-4 shows Elkanah distributing portions of the shelamim to his family at Shiloh — the offering was the occasion for the family gathering and meal.
3.3: The portions belonging to YHWH from the fellowship offering: (1) the fat covering the entrails (ḥelev hamekassah et-haqerev), (2) all the fat on the entrails, (3) the two kidneys with their fat. These internal fat deposits are the richest, most energy-dense parts of the animal. In ANE thought, the fat was the vitality of the creature and therefore the most appropriate gift to the deity. The kidneys were associated with the seat of deepest inner life and decision-making (often rendered 'heart' or 'reins' in KJV).
3.4: The fatty lobe of the liver (yoteret al-hakkaved) was significant in Mesopotamian divination (haruspicy) — liver examination was a major form of divination in Babylon and Assyria. Clay liver models from Mesopotamia have been found used for teaching divination techniques. Israel's law removes the liver from divinatory use by assigning the fatty lobe to YHWH — it is burned on the altar rather than examined for omens. This may be a deliberate polemical exclusion of divination practices.
3.5: The fat portions are burned by the priests on top of the burnt offering wood — the fellowship offering's fat shares the altar's fire with the burnt offering. The layering of offerings on the altar reflects a complex sacrificial calendar: multiple types of offerings could be in progress simultaneously, with the fellowship offering's fat additions placed on the already-burning burnt offering wood.
3.6: The flock version of the fellowship offering follows the same rules as the herd version — male or female, without defect. The consistency of the defect-free requirement (tamim) across all offering types reflects the principle that what is offered to YHWH must represent the best of what one has, not the culled or imperfect animals. Mal 1:8 later condemns the practice of offering blind, lame, or sick animals — Leviticus establishes the standard that Malachi upholds.
3.7: The lamb as fellowship offering: the same procedure, with the added rule that the fat tail (ha'alyah) of certain sheep breeds is included in YHWH's portion. The fat tail refers to the large fat deposit on the tail of the broad-tailed sheep (Ovis aries), common in the Levant — these tails can weigh several kilograms and represent a significant fat deposit. This is a breed-specific addition to the standard fat portions.
3.8: The hand-laying and slaughter before the tent of meeting — consistent with the general offering protocol. The fellowship offering's blood-sprinkling against the altar maintains the blood-manipulation at the sacred space that defines all Levitical sacrifice.
3.9: The fat tail removed close to the backbone is added to YHWH's portion in the lamb offering. The careful specification of where the tail is cut (leummat he'atset, close to the backbone) reflects the precision with which even anatomical instructions are given in Leviticus. Nothing about the offering is left to improvisation.
3.10: The two kidneys and their fat from the lamb — same as the cattle and flock rules. The standardization of YHWH's portions across all three animal categories (cattle, sheep, goat) ensures consistency regardless of species. The law is comprehensive and species-independent in its logic.
3.11: lehem isheh laYHWH: 'food of a food offering to YHWH.' The fellowship offering's fat portions are described as 'food' (lehem, bread/food) offered by fire (isheh, fire-offering). This language of feeding the deity was common in ANE temple practice — Mesopotamian temples prepared elaborate daily meals for their gods. Israel's law uses the language while implicitly denying its literal application: YHWH does not eat in the way temples literally fed their statues. The lehem isheh formula is metaphorical divine acceptance language.
3.12: The goat version of the fellowship offering — the simplest of the three, requiring no breed-specific fat tail rule. The presentation before YHWH is the same: slaughter at the sanctuary entrance, blood-sprinkling, fat burning.
3.13: The blood-sprinkling against the altar sides is consistent for goat fellowship offerings. The precision of the ritual — same blood manipulation regardless of animal — ensures that the theological content (blood at the altar = life offered before YHWH) is not diluted by variations in the offering animal.
3.14: YHWH's fat portions from the goat offering mirror those of the cattle (no fat tail, unlike the sheep). The standardized description of fat portions (entrail-covering fat, both kidneys, liver lobe) creates a consistent set of YHWH's portions across species, with the sheep exception for the fat tail being clearly marked as a breed-specific addition.
3.15: The two kidneys and liver lobe from the goat — identical to the cattle and sheep requirements. The repetition across three animal categories underscores that these specific fat organs are consistently YHWH's portion in the fellowship offering. The repetition in Leviticus (which some readers find tedious) is a legal precision device: specifying the rule for each case separately prevents legal loopholes.
3.16: kol-ḥelev laYHWH: 'All fat belongs to YHWH.' The general rule underlying all the specific fat-portion instructions: the best and richest part of every animal belongs to YHWH. The fellowship offering makes this explicit in summary form. The practical implication for the ancient Israelite: even in a celebration meal that included the offerer, YHWH's portion was the richest internal fat. This is not merely symbolic — it structured the actual food distribution at every fellowship sacrifice.
3.17: ḥuqqat olam ledoroteikhem bekol-moshevoteikhem: 'A lasting ordinance for all your generations wherever you live: you must not eat any fat or any blood.' The double prohibition on fat and blood is the most universalized law in the sacrificial code — extending beyond the land to wherever Israel lives, suggesting applicability even in diaspora contexts. This universal extension indicates these prohibitions encode something about the nature of life and holiness that transcends geography, unlike many land-specific laws.

4.1: Chapter 4 introduces the sin offering (ḥatta't) — a distinct offering type for dealing with specific categories of sin. Unlike the burnt offering (general atonement and dedication) and fellowship offering (celebration/peace), the sin offering addresses the concrete situation of having violated a specific command. The detailed structure of ch. 4 — four social classes with different requirements — reflects both a theology of graduated responsibility and a practical graduated-cost system.
4.2: beshegagah: 'unintentionally.' The sin offering applies specifically to shegagah — inadvertent, unintentional, or unknowing violations of divine commands. The deliberate parallel category is yad rama (high-handed sin, Num 15:30-31), for which there was no sacrificial remedy: the person is cut off. This distinction between inadvertent and deliberate sin is fundamental to the entire sin-offering system. Most sins covered in the Torah's sin-offering laws are acts done in ignorance, oversight, or inadvertence — not willful rebellion.
4.3: The anointed priest's sin offering: a young bull without defect (par ben-baqar tamim). The priest requires the most expensive offering because his sin has broader communal consequences — le'ashmat ha'am (bringing guilt on the people). The priestly role creates a heightened moral responsibility: the mediator's failure affects the community he mediates for. This is why Hebrews emphasizes that Jesus, unlike Levitical high priests, had no sin requiring his own offering first (Heb 7:27).
4.4: The hand-laying and slaughter procedure is the same as for burnt offerings. The sin offering's slaughter before YHWH at the sanctuary entrance gives it full covenant-transaction status: the death of the animal before YHWH is not private but official, witnessed by YHWH.
4.5: The anointed priest himself carries the blood inside the tent of meeting — the sin offering for the priest penetrates deeper into the sanctuary than most offerings. This inner-sanctuary blood manipulation is reserved for the two classes of offerer who represent the whole community (priest and congregation). The carrying of blood inside the tent models the Yom Kippur procedure in miniature.
4.6: The blood is sprinkled seven times before YHWH in front of the inner veil (parokhet haqqodesh). The sevenfold sprinkling is the completion number — seven applications before the veil that separates the holy place from the most holy. The blood reaches the boundary of the most sacred space, but does not enter it (only on Yom Kippur does blood enter the holy of holies). The spatial penetration of the blood mirrors the seriousness of the offense requiring atonement.
4.7: Some blood is applied to the four horns of the incense altar inside the tent — the altar closest to the divine presence. The horns of the altar (qarnot hamizbeaḥ) are the projecting corners that received blood in sin-offering rituals. The horn application and blood-pouring at the base of the burnt-offering altar together cover the sanctuary's altar furniture comprehensively — both inner (incense) and outer (burnt offering) altars receive blood.
4.8: The fat removal procedure for the priestly sin offering is identical to the fellowship offering (entrail fat, kidneys, liver lobe). However, unlike the fellowship offering, the priest and offerer receive no portion — the entire sin-offering animal is disposed of differently. The fat portions still belong to YHWH and are burned on the altar.
4.9: The two kidneys and liver lobe — standard YHWH portions. The sin offering's fat portions are handled exactly like the fellowship offering's, demonstrating that YHWH's portion is consistent across offering types. The difference lies not in what goes to YHWH but in what happens to the rest of the animal.
4.10: The parallel with the fellowship offering's fat removal is explicit: ka'asher yuram mish-shor zevaḥ hashlamim (just as they are removed from the ox of the fellowship offering). This cross-reference within the law is Leviticus's version of legal shorthand: rather than repeating all the anatomical details, it cites the established procedure of ch. 3. Such internal legal cross-referencing indicates a coherent legal corpus, not a random collection of rules.
4.11: The hide, flesh, head, legs, entrails, and dung of the priestly sin-offering bull — everything except the fat portions burned on the altar — must be removed entirely from the sanctuary. The complete enumeration of body parts emphasizes totality: nothing of the animal's body remains in the camp. The priestly sin offering has no human beneficiary — not offerer, not priest, no one in the camp eats from it.
4.12: The carcass is taken to the ash disposal site outside the camp (el-shar la-deshen miḥuts lammaḥaneh) and burned there entirely. The outside-the-camp disposal is significant: the most sin-laden sacrifices go furthest from the center. The carcass of the sin offering for the priest or congregation carries concentrated moral weight that requires spatial removal from the community. Hebrews 13:11-12 exploits this: Jesus suffered outside the gate to sanctify the people through his own blood, fulfilling the type of the sin-offering burned outside the camp.
4.13: The second case: the entire congregation (kol-adat Yisrael) sins unintentionally. Communal sin is possible — a wrong done collectively without individual awareness. The example is a commandment hidden from the assembly (ne'lam min-ha'eda) — they have violated something without knowing it. This addresses the reality of collective moral oversight in community life, not merely individual transgression.
4.14: The communal sin offering is the same as the priestly sin offering: a young bull. The equivalence in offering between priest and congregation reflects their parallel representational roles — just as the priest's sin affects the whole community, so the community's collective sin requires the same level of atonement. The elders represent the congregation in the laying-on-of-hands and slaughter.
4.15: The ziqne ha'eda (elders of the congregation) lay their hands on the bull's head on behalf of the whole assembly — a representative semikah. The elders' identification act extends the individual hand-laying mechanism to the corporate level: the whole congregation is present through its representative leadership. This creates a theology of corporate representation in atonement.
4.16: The anointed priest brings the community sin-offering blood inside the tent — same procedure as for his own sin offering. The priest acts as mediator for communal sin as he does for his own: the same blood-manipulation procedure covers both cases. The repetition of the inner-sanctuary blood ritual for both priestly and communal sin offerings signals that both represent the most serious impurity requiring maximum penetration toward the divine presence.
4.17: The sevenfold sprinkling before the veil — exactly as in v. 6. The parallel treatment of priestly and communal sin offerings is deliberate: both require the same level of atonement because both affect the entire covenant community. The priest's sin affects the people; the people's sin involves the priests. The two cases are mirror images.
4.18: Blood on the horns of the incense altar and poured out at the base of the burnt-offering altar — same as the priestly offering. The full altar coverage (inner incense altar and outer burnt-offering altar) is maintained for both community cases. The outside disposal of the carcass (vv. 11-12 and 21) completes the parallel — both priestly and communal sin offerings are burned outside the camp.
4.19: All fat portions are removed and burned on the altar — same as the priestly case. The consistency of procedure across the two community-level sin offerings (priest and congregation) reflects the legal principle that similar cases receive similar treatment. Leviticus 4 is organized by social rank but unified by procedural consistency within each rank-class.
4.20: vekhipper aleihem hakohen venislaḥ lahem: 'The priest makes atonement for them and they shall be forgiven.' The atonement formula of the sin offering concludes with forgiveness (sliḥah) — the communal sin offering actually achieves its goal. Unlike the Yom Kippur offering (which achieves annual comprehensive cleansing), each individual sin offering deals with specific instances of unintentional transgression. The formula venislaḥ lo/lahem (he/they shall be forgiven) closes each of the four cases in ch. 4.
4.21: The second bull is burned outside the camp — the same outside-camp disposal as the priestly sin offering (v. 12). The legal shorthand ka'asher saraf et-hapar harishon (as he burned the first bull) connects both communal offerings to the established procedure. The outside-camp burning is the defining disposal method for all sin offerings whose blood enters the tent of meeting — the most sin-laden animals go farthest from the center.
4.22: The third case: the ruler (nasi). The term nasi refers to a tribal or community leader below the priestly and communal level — a chieftain, judge, or prominent figure. His sin offering is a male goat rather than a bull — a significant step down in cost from the priestly/communal bull. The graduated scale of offerings reflects graduated communal impact: a leader's sin affects his followers, but not the whole congregation as the priest's sin does.
4.23: The ruler's male goat without defect is brought when the sin becomes known (keasher yoda'at ḥatato) — the same awareness-trigger as in the communal case. The phrase underscores that the sin offering system addresses post-hoc discovered violations, not pre-emptive moral cleansing. It is a remedial, not preventive, system.
4.24: The slaughter location for the ruler's offering is the place for burnt offerings — not inside the tent of meeting. The spatial difference between the leader's offering and the priestly/communal offerings is significant: the blood goes to the outer altar only, not inside the sanctuary. This spatial distinction reflects the lower communal impact of a leader's sin compared to a priest's or congregation's sin.
4.25: Blood on the horns of the burnt-offering altar only — not the incense altar inside the tent. The blood application goes to the altar in the court, not into the holy place. This is the key spatial distinction between the two levels of sin offering: inner-sanctuary blood manipulation (priest, congregation) versus outer-altar blood manipulation (ruler, common person). The sin offering's spatial penetration reflects the offender's communal weight.
4.26: The fat portions are burned and atonement is made for the ruler's unintentional sin. The formula closes: venislaḥ lo (he shall be forgiven). The ruler receives the same forgiveness formula as the priest and congregation — the outcome of atonement is uniform across all social levels. Graduated cost structure, uniform forgiveness result.
4.27: The fourth case: the common person (am ha'arets, lit. 'people of the land' = ordinary Israelite without special status). The common person's sin offering is a female goat — one grade lower than the ruler's male goat. The female offering is less valuable than the male, reflecting the further step down in social and communal impact.
4.28: The same awareness-trigger: keasher yoda'at ḥatato (when the sin becomes known). The sin-offering system is entirely reactive to discovered violations. The normative assumption is that the person did not know they had sinned; the offering addresses the gap between their ignorance and YHWH's holiness.
4.29: The common person lays hands and slaughters at the burnt-offering place — same as the ruler. The common person and leader share the outer-altar blood procedure, distinguishing them from the priest/congregation who require inner-sanctuary blood manipulation. The spatial organization of the court reflects the spatial organization of communal accountability.
4.30: Blood on the horns of the burnt-offering altar — same as the ruler. The four-horn application gives comprehensive coverage of the altar's sanctified points. The horns projecting at each corner were where blood was applied in all altar-horn rituals, creating a four-directional coverage of the altar's sanctified space.
4.31: Fat removal and altar burning — same as the fellowship offering's fat portions. The reiaḥ niḥoaḥ formula appears here for the sin offering — acceptance language applied to an atoning rather than celebratory offering. The sin offering is received by YHWH as a pleasing act just as the burnt and fellowship offerings are. Forgiveness formula closes: venislaḥ lo.
4.32: Alternative for the common person: a lamb instead of a female goat. The two options (goat, v. 28; lamb, v. 32) give the common person flexibility based on what animals they own. The equivalence of goat and lamb for the same offering reflects that species does not matter theologically — what matters is the proper procedure and the defect-free status.
4.33: The hand-laying and slaughter at the burnt-offering altar — same as vv. 29-30. The consistent procedure across all variations of the common-person sin offering underlines that the ritual form, not the animal species, carries the atonement meaning.
4.34: Blood on the horns of the burnt-offering altar and poured out at its base — same as vv. 30 and 25. The base-pouring of the blood after horn-application is the standard conclusion to the outer-altar blood ritual; the blood not used for horn-application goes to the ground at the altar's base, returning to the earth as the blood-life principle requires.
4.35: The lamb's fat portions are identified with the fellowship offering lamb's fat — same internal cross-reference technique as v. 10 (cattle compared to fellowship offering cattle). The formula closes identically to all other sin-offering cases: fat burned, atonement made, venislaḥ lo (he shall be forgiven). The fourfold repetition of the forgiveness formula across all four social classes emphasizes that the sin offering's purpose — forgiveness — is equally available to the priest, community, leader, and ordinary person. YHWH's atonement provision spans all social levels.

5.1: If anyone sins in that he hears a public adjuration (qol alah) to testify and though he is a witness, whether he has seen or come to know the matter, yet does not speak — the qol alah (voice/announcement of an oath) is a formal public summons to witnesses to come forward in a legal proceeding; failure to testify when one has relevant knowledge creates a specific category of communal guilt (avon). The obligation to testify truthfully is foundational in the ancient Israelite legal system (Exod 20:16; 23:1-3), and non-testimony is here treated as a legal and cultic offense requiring atonement.
5.2: Or if anyone touches any unclean thing, whether a carcass of an unclean wild animal or a carcass of unclean livestock or a carcass of unclean swarming things (sheqetz), even though he does not know it — unintentional contact with animal carcasses (Lev 11 defines the clean/unclean taxonomy) creates culpability even when the person was unaware at the time. The concept of inadvertent defilement (nefesh ki tiggah) reflects an understanding that impurity has objective effects regardless of subjective intention, a feature of Israelite purity theology that distinguishes it from moral-intent-based systems like the Roman mens rea framework.
5.3: Or if he touches human uncleanness, of whatever sort the uncleanness may be, with which one becomes unclean (tum'at adam), and it is hidden from him — human impurity sources (corpse, discharge, skin disease) are distinguished from animal impurity sources (v.2); tum'at adam encompasses the full range of Lev 11–15 human impurity categories. The formula vene'elam mimenu (it was hidden from him) is the technical term for the unwitting aspect of these violations throughout Lev 4–5.
5.4: Or if anyone utters with his lips a rash oath (nishba levatte bisetafayim) to do evil or to do good, any sort of rash oath that people swear, and it is hidden from him — the rash oath (shevuat shav) is an impulsive, unconsidered verbal commitment later forgotten; the inclusion of oaths to do good (not only harmful oaths) indicates that the culpability is in the making-and-forgetting rather than in the content. The pairing 'evil or good' creates a merism covering all possible oath content.
5.5: When he becomes guilty in any of these, he shall confess the sin he has committed (ve-hitvaddah asher chata aleiha) — the confession requirement (viduy) precedes the offering; the ritual alone without acknowledgment of specific wrongdoing is insufficient. Confession before sacrifice is a distinctive feature of Israelite atonement theology; in Mesopotamian ritual texts (Marduk-šum-uṣur incantations, namburbi rituals) the verbal component exists but is typically addressed to deities in third person rather than first-person confession of specific acts.
5.6: He shall bring to YHWH as his compensation for the sin that he has committed, a female from the flock, a lamb or a goat, for a sin offering (chatat); and the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin — the standard chatat for these three categories is a female animal (contrast the male animal required for leaders and priests in Lev 4:3,22-23). The female animal represents the base-level offering for individual lay Israelites; the graduated scale that follows (vv.7-13) accommodates economic inability to provide even this.
5.7: But if he cannot afford a lamb (im lo taggiya yado dei she), then he shall bring to YHWH as his compensation for the sin that he has committed two turtledoves or two pigeons (shtei torim o shtei vnei yona) — the sliding scale begins: those who cannot afford a sheep bring two birds instead. This graduated provision for the poor is one of the most distinctive features of the Levitical offering system; no comparable sliding scale appears in Mesopotamian temple records from Ur III or Old Babylonian periods, where temple offerings were largely fixed by professional category and social rank.
5.8: And he shall bring them to the priest, who shall offer first the one for the sin offering (chatat). He shall wring its head from its neck but shall not sever it completely (malaq et-rosho mimul orfo ve-lo yavdil) — bird sin offering technique: the priest wrings the bird's neck incompletely (meliqa), a method unique to bird offerings and distinct from the slaughter (shechita) used for quadrupeds. The blood is then daubed on the altar (v.9) rather than sprinkled or poured as with larger animals.
5.9: He shall sprinkle some of the blood of the sin offering on the side of the altar, and the rest of the blood shall be drained out at the base of the altar — the bird's smaller blood volume means only partial sprinkling on the altar side, with the remainder drained at the base; the base-drainage parallels the quadruped sin offering procedure where blood is poured at the altar base after the manipulations (Lev 4:25,30,34).
5.10: The second he shall offer as a burnt offering according to the rule — the second bird serves as a burnt offering after the sin offering bird; the pairing (sin offering + burnt offering) from two birds is an economic version of the two-animal offering given by the wealthy person. The sequence sin offering before burnt offering is standard throughout Leviticus (cf. Lev 14:19-20; Num 6:16-17).
5.11: But if he cannot afford two turtledoves or two pigeons (im lo taggiya yado lishetei torim), then he who sinned shall bring for his offering a tenth of an ephah of fine flour (issaron solet) for a sin offering — the second tier of graduation: those who cannot afford even two birds bring 1/10 ephah (~2.3 liters) of fine flour. The flour offering without oil or frankincense (v.11b) marks it as penitential rather than celebratory; normal grain offerings include oil (Lev 2:4-7) and frankincense (Lev 2:15).
5.12: He shall bring it to the priest, and the priest shall take a handful of it as its memorial portion (azkaratah) and burn this on the altar, on top of the LORD's food offerings — the handful (kometz) burned as the azkarah (memorial portion) is the standard grain offering procedure (Lev 2:2), applied here to the flour chatat. The remainder goes to the priest (v.13). The azkarah (memorial/token) represents the whole offering presented to YHWH; the priestly portion represents the priest's share in the atonement transaction.
5.13: Thus the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin that he has committed in any one of these things, and he shall be forgiven. And the remainder shall be for the priest, as in the grain offering — the atonement-and-forgiveness formula is identical whether the person brought a lamb, two birds, or flour; the economic tier does not diminish the efficacy of the atonement. The graduated scale is arguably the most explicitly compassionate provision in the entire sacrificial system, ensuring that poverty is no barrier to atonement.
5.14: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — a new revelation formula separates the guilt offering (asham) legislation (vv.14-19) from the graduated sin offering (vv.1-13). The asham is a distinct sacrifice type from the chatat with different legal triggers and different restitution requirements.
5.15: If anyone commits a breach of faith (ma'al) and sins unintentionally in any of the holy things of YHWH (miqdeshei YHWH), he shall bring to YHWH as his compensation (ashmato) a ram without blemish from the flock, valued at silver shekels — the asham is triggered by ma'al (sacrilege, breach of faith against holy things), the failure to give YHWH what is rightly his: tithes, firstfruits, vow payments, priestly dues. The requirement for a ram of silver valuation distinguishes the asham from the chatat; a monetary equivalence must be established, reflecting the reparation (rather than mere atonement) character of the guilt offering.
5.16: He shall also make restitution for what he has done amiss in the holy thing and shall add a fifth to it (yosif chamishito alav) and give it to the priest — the chomesh (one-fifth surcharge on restitution) is distinctive to the asham system; no sliding scale applies here because the violation involved specific sacred property with assessable value. In Hammurabi's Code (§§5, 120, 124), restitution requirements vary from two-fold to ten-fold; the Levitical one-fifth surcharge is more modest but applied consistently across all asham contexts. The surcharge goes to the priest as part of the sacred property system.
5.17: If anyone sins, doing any of the things that by YHWH's commandments ought not to be done, though he did not know it (ve-hu lo yada), and realizes his guilt (ve-ashem), he shall bear his iniquity — the guilt offering for general unwitting violations (not limited to holy things as in v.15): the person who later realizes they may have transgressed brings the asham as a precautionary measure. This 'floating guilt' concept has no direct ANE parallel; Mesopotamian texts address known violations rather than suspected-but-uncertain ones.
5.18: He shall bring to the priest a ram without blemish from the flock, or its equivalent, for a guilt offering. The priest shall make atonement for him for the mistake that he made unintentionally, and he shall be forgiven — the same ram requirement as v.15; the shegagah (error, mistake) term used here and in Lev 4:2 covers the full range of unintentional violations. The asham for unwitting violation in v.17-19 (the 'doubtful asham') was extensively developed in rabbinic law as the asham talui (suspended guilt offering).
5.19: It is a guilt offering (asham hu); he has indeed incurred guilt before YHWH — the declaration asham hu formally categorizes the offering and confirms the person's legal status as guilty. The emphatic infinitive absolute pattern (asham asham in the Hebrew sense) reinforces the certainty of guilt even in the case of uncertainty about the specific act.

6.1: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — ch 6 opens a new section with a second revelation formula; in the Hebrew Bible the chapter division begins what rabbinic tradition calls the 'priestly torah' (torat ha-kohanim) section, containing detailed instructions for the priests' execution of each sacrifice type. Vv.1-7 (Heb. 5:20-26) address the asham for interpersonal fraud before returning to the altar-service rubrics.
6.2: If anyone sins and commits a breach of faith against YHWH by deceiving his neighbor in a matter of deposit or security (piqqadon o vitesumeth yad), or through robbery, or if he has oppressed his neighbor — the ma'al (breach of faith) category expands here to cover interpersonal wrongs that have a perjury dimension: false denial of a deposit, fraud regarding a pledge, theft, oppression, and false oath. The asham's trigger is the false oath by which the perpetrator invoked YHWH's name to conceal the wrongdoing (v.3), which transforms an interpersonal tort into a sacred violation.
6.3: Or has found something lost and lied about it, swearing falsely (nishba al-shaqer) — the false oath is the hinge that connects the interpersonal wrong to YHWH: by swearing falsely in YHWH's name, the offender has made YHWH complicit in the lie, creating a sacrilege dimension that triggers the asham mechanism alongside any civil restitution. The Decalogue's prohibition of false swearing (Exod 20:7) is enacted here through the asham procedure.
6.4: If he has sinned and has realized his guilt, he shall restore what he took by robbery or what he got by oppression or the deposit that was committed to him or the lost thing that he found — the sequence of restoration (hashiv) requires returning the specific item or its equivalent; the civil and cultic restoration are inseparable. The rabbinic concept that the stolen item must be returned before the sacrifice is accepted (b. Bava Kamma 94b) derives from this structure.
6.5: Or anything about which he has sworn falsely; he shall restore it in full (berosho) and shall add a fifth to it (chomishitav yosif alav), and give it to him to whom it belongs on the day he realizes his guilt — the chomesh (one-fifth surcharge) applies to interpersonal restitution as to sacrilege against holy things (5:16); the surcharge must be delivered on the same day as the acknowledgment of guilt, preventing delay. The precision about timing (day of guilt-realization) reflects the system's concern with the quality of repentance, not merely formal legal compliance.
6.6: And he shall bring to the priest as his compensation to YHWH a ram without blemish from the flock, or its equivalent, for a guilt offering — the same ram requirement as Lev 5:15,18; the asham for interpersonal fraud uses the identical animal as the asham for sacrilege against holy things, confirming that false swearing in YHWH's name elevates interpersonal tort into the sacred category. The civil restitution (vv.4-5) and the cultic offering (v.6-7) are both required; neither alone is sufficient.
6.7: And the priest shall make atonement for him before YHWH, and he shall be forgiven for any of the things that one may do and thereby become guilty — the comprehensive forgiveness formula: whatever the specific fraud category (robbery, false deposit denial, lost-property fraud, false oath), the atonement mechanism is the same. The breadth of the formula reflects the chatat/asham system's capacity to address the full range of unwitting and acknowledged violations.
6.8: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — the third revelation formula in chs 5–7, introducing the priestly service rubrics for the burnt offering (vv.9-13). These instructions address what the priests do with offerings that have been presented, whereas chs 1–5 address what the offerers present.
6.9: Command Aaron and his sons, saying, This is the law (torah) of the burnt offering (olah). The burnt offering shall be on the hearth (al-moqedah) on the altar all night until the morning, and the fire of the altar shall be kept burning on it — the tamid (continual) principle: the olah placed on the altar in the evening must remain burning through the night. The word moqed (hearth, burning-place) describes the altar's fire-bearing surface; the all-night burning allows complete combustion of the evening sacrifice before the morning's ashes are removed.
6.10: And the priest shall put on his linen garment (mido bad) and put his linen undergarments on his body, and he shall take up the ashes (deshen) to which the fire has reduced the burnt offering on the altar and put them beside the altar — the priestly linen garments (Exod 28:40-43; Lev 16:4) are required even for the mundane ash-removal; contact with the altar's sacred space demands vestments. The deshen (ashes/fatty remnants) of the burnt offering are treated as sacred residue; they are not simply swept away but placed respectfully beside the altar before being carried outside the camp (v.11).
6.11: Then he shall take off his garments and put on other garments and carry the ashes outside the camp to a clean place (makom tahor) — the change of garments for ash-carrying indicates a two-stage holiness gradient: the altar area requires the most sacred garments; the task of carrying ashes outside requires different (lesser?) garments. The makom tahor (clean place) is outside the camp — not the unclean zone where diseased persons and waste are disposed, but a specifically designated clean area for sacred remnants. Later rabbinic law developed the beit ha-deshen (ash-house) from this provision.
6.12: The fire on the altar shall be kept burning on it; it shall not go out. The priest shall burn wood on it every morning, and he shall arrange the burnt offering on it and shall burn on it the fat of the peace offerings — the fire's perpetuity (lo tikhbeh, it shall not go out) is stated three times in vv.12-13; the eternal flame is the sign of YHWH's permanent presence and ongoing acceptance of Israel's worship. In the ancient Near East, temple fires were also carefully maintained; the Mesopotamian sacred brazier (dipāru) was kept burning in major temples, but the Levitical emphasis on the fire's divine origin (Lev 9:24) and permanent maintenance is more theologically charged.
6.13: Fire shall be kept burning on the altar continually (tamid); it shall not go out — the single word tamid (continually, perpetually) becomes the technical term for all the regular, unending acts of sanctuary service: the olat tamid (continual burnt offering, Exod 29:42), the menorah tamid (continual lamp, Exod 27:20), the lechem ha-panim (bread of presence, displayed continually). The perpetual altar fire is the heartbeat of the sanctuary's operation.
6.14: And this is the law of the grain offering (torat ha-minchah). The sons of Aaron shall offer it before YHWH in front of the altar — the grain offering rubric transitions from burnt offering to the minchah; the grain offering can be presented independently (Lev 2) or as an accompaniment to animal sacrifices (Lev 7:11-14; Num 15). The priestly torah for the minchah addresses the handling after presentation: the azkarah burning and the priestly portions.
6.15: And one of them shall take from it a handful of the fine flour of the grain offering and of its oil and all the frankincense that is on the grain offering and burn this as its memorial portion (azkaratah) on the altar, a pleasing aroma to YHWH — the kometz azkarah (handful as memorial/token) burns on the altar as the YHWH-portion; the remainder is the priestly food. The term azkarah (memorial, reminder) has generated significant debate: does it remind YHWH of the offerer, or does it serve as the offerer's memorial before YHWH? Both senses may be active.
6.16: And the remainder of it Aaron and his sons shall eat. It shall be eaten unleavened (matzot te'achel) in a holy place — priestly eating of the grain offering remainder is a sacred act in a sacred place; the requirement for unleavened (matzot) grain echoes the Passover prohibition on leaven and the general Levitical principle that leaven does not belong in fire-offerings to YHWH (Lev 2:11). The association of leaven with fermentation/corruption may underlie the restriction.
6.17: It shall not be baked with leaven. I have given it as their portion from my food offerings. It is most holy (qodesh qedashim hi), like the sin offering and the guilt offering — the category qodesh qedashim (most holy) restricts consumption to male priests only (cf. v.18) within the sanctuary precincts; this is the highest sanctity category in Levitical law, shared by the sin offering (chatat), guilt offering (asham), and grain offering (minchah).
6.18: Every male among the children of Aaron may eat of it, as decreed forever throughout your generations, from YHWH's food offerings. Whoever touches them shall become holy (kol asher yigga bahem yiqdash) — the touching-becomes-holy principle: contact with most-holy objects consecrates whatever (whoever) touches them. This is the opposite of impurity transmission; impurity defiles, holiness consecrates. The principle also means that unauthorized touching of most-holy objects would result in dangerous overexposure to holiness — which is why contact is restricted to authorized priests.
6.19: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — a fourth revelation formula introduces the ordination grain offering (minchat chinuch, the offering for Aaron and his sons, vv.20-23).
6.20: This is the offering that Aaron and his sons shall offer to YHWH on the day when he is anointed: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a regular grain offering, half of it in the morning and half in the evening — the high priest's daily grain offering (minchat chavitin) consists of 1/10 ephah divided into morning and evening portions, offered on a griddle, served fully to YHWH without any priestly retention. This distinguishes the high priest's offering from the lay grain offering (where only the handful burns and priests eat the rest): priestly self-offering is total, not partial.
6.21: It shall be made with oil on a griddle (al-machabat bashemen ta'aseh). You shall bring it well mixed, in baked pieces like a grain offering, and offer it for a pleasing aroma to YHWH — the preparation method (griddle with oil) for the daily priestly minchah parallels the lay griddle offering of Lev 2:5; the murcheshet (well-stirred, broken pieces) ensures even cooking. The Talmud (Menachot 78a) reconstructs the distinctive folded-and-griddle-fried preparation of the chavitin.
6.22: The anointed priest who is in his place among his sons shall offer it. It is a perpetual due; it shall be wholly burned to YHWH (kalil le-YHWH tiqtár) — every successor high priest continues the father's daily offering; the requirement perpetuates through the priestly genealogy. Kalil (wholly burned, complete) confirms that unlike the lay grain offering (from which priests eat a portion), the priestly grain offering is entirely consumed on the altar — a fitting symbol of priestly self-consecration.
6.23: Every grain offering of a priest shall be wholly burned. It shall not be eaten — the prohibition on priestly eating of their own grain offerings parallels the principle that the priests who handle sin offerings cannot eat their own sin offerings (Lev 6:30): the officiating priest cannot be simultaneously the beneficiary of his own sacrificial transaction.
6.24: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — fifth revelation formula; the sin offering (chatat) priestly rubric (vv.25-30) addresses consumption and handling of the most-holy sin offering blood and meat.
6.25: Speak to Aaron and his sons, saying, This is the law of the sin offering (torat ha-chatat). In the place where the burnt offering is killed, the sin offering shall be killed before YHWH; it is most holy — the slaughter location (north side of the altar, Lev 1:11) is the same for sin offering and burnt offering, preventing visual distinction between the most-solemn and the standard offerings. The most-holy status means the slaughter space itself is consecrated during the procedure.
6.26: The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it. In a holy place it shall be eaten, in the court of the tent of meeting (bachatzer ohel mo'ed) — the sin offering meat is priestly food, consumed within the sanctuary court; the priest who performs the atonement eats the offering, creating a physical identification between the priest and the sin he has borne for the offerer. The eating-as-bearing-sin motif (Lev 10:17: 'you should eat it in the holy place, since it is most holy and was given to you that you may bear the iniquity of the congregation') is explicit in later texts.
6.27: Whatever touches its flesh shall be holy, and when any of its blood is sprinkled on a garment, you shall wash that on which it was sprinkled in a holy place — the most-holy garment-staining rule: blood from a sin offering contaminates garments and must be laundered in the sacred precincts (not simply anywhere). The holiness-contamination of the blood (not mere impurity) reflects the concentrated sacred power of the chatat's blood, which has been applied to the altar horns or to the veil in the atoning process.
6.28: And the earthen vessel in which it is boiled shall be broken (keli cheres asher tevushal bo yishaver), and if it is boiled in a bronze vessel, that shall be scoured and rinsed in water — the earthenware-vs-bronze distinction for contaminated vessels: earthenware absorbs the sacred residue permanently and must be destroyed; bronze can be cleaned by scouring and rinsing.
