The unchanged mark despite washing indicates the contamination is structural (embedded in the fiber) rather than superficial (a removable deposit). The additional assessment 'whether the spot has eaten into the back or front of the garment' evaluates the penetration depth: has the contamination reached the underside? Penetrating contamination is more severe than surface contamination and may require burning even without active spreading.
13.56: If the mark has faded after washing → the priest tears out the affected part from the garment, leather, warp, or woof. The surgical excision of the affected area is the moderate outcome between full burn (v. 52) and full cleaning (v. 58): when the contamination has responded to washing (faded) but not fully resolved, removing only the affected section salvages the rest of the article. This partial-destruction option reflects economic pragmatism: destroying an expensive garment entirely when only a section is affected would impose unnecessary economic hardship.
13.57: If the contamination reappears after section removal → burn the entire article. A spreading condition that returns after surgical section removal has demonstrated that the contamination cannot be contained by partial removal. The sequence (wash → section removal → reappearance) demonstrates a persistent, aggressive biological growth that requires complete destruction. The escalating response (quarantine → wash → section removal → total burn) mirrors modern protocols for persistent fungal contamination in textiles and food storage facilities.
13.58: If washing fully removes the mark → wash a second time and declare the article clean. The double-washing requirement for articles that have cleared after the first wash ensures that the cleaning is thorough before returning the article to use. The confirmation washing prevents premature re-introduction of articles whose contamination may have only superficially cleared. The requirement also has the practical effect of standardizing the cleaning protocol: every cleaned fabric article must be washed twice before being declared clean and returned to use.
13.59: The closing formula: 'This is the law (zot torat nega ha-tzara'at) for a skin disease mark on a garment of wool or linen, in the warp or woof, or on any leather article — to pronounce it clean or to declare it unclean.' The closing zot torat formula (identical to the closing of ch. 7, 11, 12, and appearing at the end of ch. 14 for the full tzara'at law) marks a literary-legal conclusion. The binary outcome — clean or unclean — is explicitly stated as the purpose of the entire elaborate inspection system: the system exists to produce a definitive, authoritative verdict.

17.1: And YHWH spoke to Moses — the new revelation formula opens a new legislative unit; ch 17 addresses the blood-slaughter-centralization laws that bridge the Yom Kippur materials and the Holiness Code proper (chs 17–26). The chapter's concern with blood (vv.3-16) and with the theological basis of the prohibition creates one of the most important theological passages in Leviticus.
17.2: Speak to Aaron and his sons and to all the people of Israel and say to them — the expanded address (Aaron, his sons, all Israel) marks this as a comprehensive law applying to all, not only priests; the blood legislation transcends cultic categories and governs the entire community's relationship to blood.
17.3: If any one of the house of Israel kills an ox or a lamb or a goat in the camp, or kills it outside the camp — the prohibition covers all slaughter of the three sacrificial species (ox, sheep, goat) both inside and outside the camp. This is the centralization law: all slaughter of these animals must be brought to the sanctuary, not performed in private. The 'outside the camp' specification covers the wilderness context; in the land, the parallel rule will be adapted (Deut 12 permits non-sacrificial slaughter at any location but requires all sacrifice at the central sanctuary).
17.4: And does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it as an offering to YHWH in front of the tabernacle of YHWH, bloodguilt shall be imputed to that man (dam yechashev la-ish ha-hu) — unauthorized slaughter of sacrificial animals incurs bloodguilt (dam) as though the person had shed human blood. The severity of the penalty (karet, v.4b) reflects the theological claim that all slaughter of domestic animals belongs to YHWH's sacrificial economy; private killing outside the sanctuary denies YHWH's ownership of animal life.
17.5: This is to the end that the people of Israel may bring their sacrifices that they sacrifice in the open field (al-peney ha-sadeh), that they may bring them to YHWH, to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the purpose clause: the law drives all field-slaughter into the sanctuary system. The 'open field' sacrifices are the unauthorized alternative that the centralization law prohibits; the phrase implies that pre-centralization or non-Israelite practice involved field slaughter without priestly mediation.
17.6: And the priest shall throw the blood on the altar of YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting and burn the fat for a pleasing aroma to YHWH — the re-categorized field slaughter becomes a peace offering (shelamim) with standard blood and fat procedures; the formerly unauthorized killing is transformed into legitimate communal worship. This economically validates the centralization law: it is not confiscatory but transformative, integrating all animal slaughter into the covenant fellowship-meal structure.
17.7: So they shall no more sacrifice their sacrifices to goat demons (la-se'irim asher hem zonim achareihem), after whom they whore. This shall be a statute forever for them throughout their generations — the se'irim (goat-demons, satyrs) are the wilderness spirit-beings to whom unauthorized field sacrifices were directed; the prostitution language (zonim, whoring) frames these offerings as spiritual adultery. The se'irim appear in Isa 13:21 and 34:14 as wilderness spirits; Lev 17:7's reference implies that such field-sacrifices to goat-demons were an actual practice in Israel's wilderness experience (cf. the golden calf incident, Amos 5:25-26).
17.8: And you shall say to them, Any one of the house of Israel, or of the strangers who sojourn among them, who offers a burnt offering or sacrifice — the centralization law extends to sojourning Gentiles residing in Israel; they are equally prohibited from unauthorized sacrifice. The inclusion of the ger (sojourner) in the same legislation as Israel is consistent throughout Lev 17 (vv.10,12,13,15) and reflects the Holiness Code's principle that the land itself requires protection from all defilement, regardless of the offender's ethnic identity.
17.9: And does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it to YHWH, that man shall be cut off from his people — karet for unauthorized sacrifice applies equally to Israelite and sojourner; the sanctity of the centralized sanctuary system is non-negotiable for all who live within the covenant community's territory.
17.10: If any one of the house of Israel or of the strangers who sojourn among them eats any blood, I will set my face against that person who eats blood and will cut him off from among his people — the blood-eating prohibition is restated with the divine first-person threat (natati panay, I will set my face against) that signals YHWH's direct punitive action rather than communal execution. The prohibition applies universally; the following verse (v.11) provides the theological rationale.
17.11: For the life (nefesh) of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls, for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life (ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhapper) — the foundational theological statement of Levitical sacrifice: blood contains/is the nefesh (life/soul) of the flesh; YHWH has given blood specifically for the altar-atonement function; and it is the blood (qua life-substance) that performs atonement. This three-part statement explains why blood is simultaneously the most sacred substance (YHWH's atonement-medium) and the most prohibited food (it belongs to YHWH). The blood-nefesh identification has no parallel in ANE sacrificial theology; Mesopotamian texts describe gods consuming food offerings but do not articulate a blood-as-life-for-atonement theology.
17.12: Therefore I have said to the people of Israel, No person among you shall eat blood, neither shall any stranger who sojourns among you eat blood — the deductive conclusion from v.11: because blood is nefesh given for atonement, it may not be eaten. The logical structure (theological premise → behavioral prohibition) is explicit, making Lev 17:11 the most clearly reasoned prohibition in the Levitical law code.
17.13: Any one also of the people of Israel, or of the strangers who sojourn among them, who takes in hunting any beast or bird that may be eaten shall pour out its blood and cover it with earth (shefakh dam vekissahu be-afar) — the game-animal blood rule: hunted animals (not sacrificeable) still require blood respect; their blood must be poured out and covered with earth (not swallowed, not left exposed). The earth-covering creates a kind of minimal 'burial' of the life-substance; the action acknowledges that even the life of a wild animal belongs to YHWH and cannot simply be consumed or wasted.
17.14: For the life of every creature is its blood: its blood is its life. Therefore I have said to the people of Israel, You shall not eat the blood of any creature, for the life of every creature is its blood. Whoever eats it shall be cut off — the restatement of v.11's principle extended to all creatures (not only domestic sacrificial animals): nefesh-in-blood is universal. The double formulation (ki nefesh kol-basar damo beenafesho hu) and the triple repetition of the nefesh-blood identification within vv.11-14 emphasize that this is the core theological claim of the chapter.
17.15: And every person who eats what dies of itself (neveilah) or what is torn by beasts (terefah), whether he is a native or a sojourner, shall wash his clothes and bathe himself in water and be unclean until the evening; then he shall be clean — eating neveilah or terefah creates until-evening impurity (not karet); these animals died without proper blood-drainage through slaughter. The lesser penalty than blood-eating reflects that the prohibition is still operative but the blood has not been deliberately consumed in the same sense as fresh blood eating.
17.16: But if he does not wash them or bathe his flesh, he shall bear his iniquity — the penalty for failure to perform the required laundering-and-bathing purification after eating neveilah/terefah; bearing iniquity (avono yissa) is a lesser consequence than karet but still indicates personal culpability for ignoring a known purification requirement.

18.1: And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — the new revelation formula opens the most extensive sexual-ethics legislation in the Torah; Lev 18 is the foundational document of Israelite sexual ethics, framed by the Egypt/Canaan contrast (vv.2-5,24-30) and structured around the ervah (nakedness) motif.
18.2: Speak to the people of Israel and say to them, I am YHWH your God — the divine self-identification formula (ani YHWH Eloheichem) grounds the sexual ethics in covenant identity before any specific prohibition; the motivation for the following legislation is not primarily social utility or natural law but covenant loyalty to the one who brought Israel out of Egypt. The Holiness Code (chs 17–26) opens and closes every major section with variations of this formula.
18.3: You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt, where you lived, and you shall not do as they do in the land of Canaan, to which I am bringing you. You shall not walk in their statutes — the negative framing by two geographical-cultural poles: Egypt (the past of slavery) and Canaan (the future of conquest). Both cultures serve as negative examples of sexual practice; the framing of Israelite ethics as a third way between two known systems is structurally similar to the Deuteronomic contrast between Egypt and Canaan (Deut 11:10; 17:16). Egyptian records show no comprehensive sexual prohibition code; Ugaritic texts from Canaan similarly show no equivalent to the Levitical list.
18.4: You shall follow my rules and keep my statutes and walk in them. I am YHWH your God — the positive counterpart to v.3's negative: instead of Egyptian/Canaanite statutes, Israel is to follow YHWH's mishpatim (judgments) and chuqqim (statutes). The self-identification formula (ani YHWH Eloheichem) repeated from v.2 creates a bracketing emphasis: the entire legislative section hangs on covenant identity.
18.5: You shall therefore keep my statutes and my rules; if a person does them, he shall live by them (va-chai bahem): I am YHWH — the promise of life through the statutes is one of the most important statements in Leviticus; Paul cites it in Gal 3:12 and Rom 10:5 as the principle of the law-covenant (life through doing), which he contrasts with faith-righteousness. Ezekiel 20:11,13,21 also cites this verse as the foundational covenant life-promise.
18.6: None of you shall approach any one of his close relatives to uncover nakedness (lo tiqrevu lechol-she'er besaro legalot ervah). I am YHWH — she'er (flesh-kin) covers blood relatives and relatives by marriage; galot ervah (uncover nakedness) is the chapter's central euphemism for sexual intercourse within prohibited relationships. The proximity of kinship is the defining criterion; the ervah framework defines the family as a protected zone of non-sexual relationships.
18.7: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father, which is the nakedness of your mother; she is your mother, you shall not uncover her nakedness — the mother is doubly protected: directly (she is your mother) and indirectly (her sexuality belongs to the father). The equivalence 'father's nakedness = mother's nakedness' (v.8 separates these for the stepmother case) indicates that the first prohibition covers the biological mother primarily as the father's wife.
18.8: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife; it is your father's nakedness — the stepmother case (father's wife who is not biological mother): the prohibition is stated separately, framed as a violation of the father's domain rather than only the woman's personhood. Reuben's violation (Gen 35:22; 49:3-4) is the paradigmatic case; 1 Cor 5:1 identifies a Corinthian case of this violation as the reason for Paul's most severe church discipline.
18.9: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your sister, your father's daughter or your mother's daughter, whether brought up in the family or in another home — the sister prohibition covers full sisters (same father and mother) and half-sisters on either side; the phrase 'brought up in another home' (born outside the household) closes a potential loophole for half-siblings raised separately. Tamar's hope in 2 Sam 13:13 that David might give her to Amnon in marriage suggests that at some point half-sibling marriage was considered possible (or Tamar was desperate); the Levitical prohibition is absolute.
18.10: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your son's daughter or of your daughter's daughter, for their nakedness is your own nakedness — grandchild incest is prohibited by derivation: one's grandchild carries one's own flesh (ki ervatekha hennah, their nakedness is your nakedness). The generational extension of kinship ties brings grandchildren under the same protective framework as children.
18.11: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife's daughter, brought up by your father, since she is your sister — stepsister (father's wife's daughter from a previous marriage, not biologically related): prohibited by the family-relationship created by the father-stepmother marriage, even without blood relation. The relational/social kinship created by marriage is treated as equally prohibitive as biological kinship in several of these cases.
18.12: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's sister; she is your father's near kinswoman — paternal aunt prohibition; the term she'er (flesh-kin, near kinswoman) names the basis for the prohibition. The paternal aunt case appears in the Moses genealogy: Amram married his father's sister Jochebed (Exod 6:20; Num 26:59), a pre-Sinai marriage that the Levitical legislation would prohibit. The law's retrospective inapplicability to Moses's parents is implicit in the Sinai-origin formula of Lev 7:38.
18.13: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother's sister, for she is your mother's near kinswoman — maternal aunt prohibition; symmetrical with v.12. The two aunt prohibitions (paternal and maternal) create comprehensive coverage of the parents' siblings.
18.14: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's brother, that is, you shall not approach his wife; she is your aunt — paternal uncle's wife prohibition (not the uncle himself, as the sexual category concerns the man-woman relationships in this framework); the dodah (aunt, uncle's wife) is the prohibited woman, not the uncle's own person. The affinal (by marriage) kinship is treated as prohibitive alongside consanguineal (by blood) kinship.
18.15: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your daughter-in-law; she is your son's wife, you shall not uncover her nakedness — daughter-in-law prohibition; the double rationale (she is your daughter-in-law + she is your son's wife) emphasizes both the generational relationship and the specific marital bond. Judah's violation with Tamar (Gen 38:15-18) — even though done in ignorance — is resolved through a complex genealogical providential narrative; the Levitical law makes the prohibition explicit.
18.16: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your brother's wife; it is your brother's nakedness — brother's wife prohibition (with the exception of levirate marriage in Deut 25:5-10 for a childless brother). The Herodias case (Mark 6:17-18; Matt 14:3-4) — John the Baptist's rebuke of Herod Antipas for marrying his brother's wife Herodias while the brother (Philip) was still living — represents the precise violation of Lev 18:16, and John's rebuke draws on this legislation.
18.17: You shall not uncover the nakedness of a woman and of her daughter, and you shall not take her son's daughter or her daughter's daughter to uncover her nakedness; they are relatives; it is depravity (zimmah hi) — the three-generation prohibition: a woman, her daughter, and her granddaughter may not all be wives of the same man (not simultaneously and not serially). Zimmah (deliberate wickedness, depravity) is the strongest evaluative term in the chapter; this violation receives the burning death penalty in Lev 20:14.
18.18: And you shall not take a woman as a rival wife to her sister, uncovering her nakedness while her sister is still alive — the rival-sisters prohibition (not a permanent ban on marrying a deceased wife's sister, but on having two sisters simultaneously as wives): the Jacob/Leah/Rachel case is the obvious background; the legislation formalizes what the narrative already portrayed as a source of profound family dysfunction. The prohibition targets the tzarar (rivalry, vexation) that polygamous sister-marriages create.
18.19: You shall not approach a woman to uncover her nakedness while she is in her menstrual uncleanness (niddah) — the niddah prohibition (cf. Lev 15:19-24; 20:18) placed within the sexual prohibition list connects menstrual impurity to the broader ervah framework; sexual intercourse during niddah uncovers the woman's nakedness in her most vulnerable and ritually restricted state.
18.20: And you shall not lie sexually with your neighbor's wife and so make yourself unclean with her — adultery (Lev 20:10: capital offense for both parties) is briefly stated in the ervah framework; the tum'ah (impurity) consequence for the man is noted here. The brevity of the adultery prohibition relative to the incest prohibitions reflects that adultery was already comprehensively addressed in the Decalogue (Exod 20:14) and other legislation.
18.21: You shall not give any of your children to offer them to Molech, and so profane the name of your God: I am YHWH — Molech sacrifice in a sexual-prohibition chapter is contextually anomalous but theologically coherent: both groups of laws concern misuse of the life-generating capacities of the family; Molech-sacrifice is the ultimate perversion of parental relation (giving children to death rather than life), placed at the center of the chapter's list. Molech was a deity associated with child-sacrifice in Ammon and Canaan (2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 32:35; 1 Kgs 11:7); the practice is attested archaeologically in the topheth sacrificial precinct outside Jerusalem (Gehenna/Ben Hinnom valley).
18.22: You shall not lie with a male as with a woman (mishkevei isha); it is an abomination (to'evah) — to'evah (abomination, something YHWH detests) is applied to several violations in Lev 18 and 20 (vv.26,29,30; 20:13); it is not a unique categorical term applied only to this prohibition but a severity marker applied to violations that fall outside YHWH's created order. The mishkevei isha (lyings of a woman) formula specifies the sexual category being prohibited by reference to its female analog.
18.23: And you shall not lie with any animal and so make yourself unclean with it, neither shall any woman give herself to an animal to lie with it: it is perversion (tevel) — bestiality (Lev 20:15-16: capital for both man and woman) is the closing prohibition of the sexual list; tevel (confusion, mixing of kinds, violation of species boundaries) is the evaluative term, the same used in Lev 19:19 for hybrid-kind violations (crossbreeding, mixed seed, mixed fabric). The common thread: violations of created categories are tevel; violations of YHWH's sanctity are to'evah.
18.24: Do not make yourselves unclean by any of these things, for by all these the nations I am driving out before you have made themselves unclean — the closing frame (vv.24-30) returns to the Egypt/Canaan contrast of vv.1-5; the Canaanite nations' defilement through these practices is the reason YHWH is expelling them. The sexual violations are not merely interpersonal wrongs but land-defiling acts with territorial consequences.
18.25: And the land became unclean, so that I punished its iniquity upon it, and the land vomited out its inhabitants (vataqe ha-aretz et-yoshveiha) — the land-vomiting metaphor: the land has a quasi-organic response to moral pollution; it cannot contain the accumulated defilement and expels its population as a contaminated stomach expels its contents. This is one of the most theologically distinctive concepts in the Holiness Code; the land is not inert but responsive to the moral quality of its inhabitants' behavior.
18.26: But you shall keep my statutes and my rules and do none of these abominations, either the native or the stranger who sojourns among you — the covenant counter-command: Israel and its sojourners are to follow YHWH's laws, not the Canaanite practices. The inclusion of the sojourner (ger) in both prohibition and command confirms that the land's holiness requirement applies to all who inhabit it, not only to covenant-Israel.
18.27: For the people of the land, who were before you, did all of these abominations, so that the land became unclean — the historical basis for the legislation: the Canaanites' sexual violations produced the land's defilement that now requires their expulsion. Israel is being brought into a land that has already been through one cycle of defilement-and-expulsion; the warning is experientially grounded in the history of the land itself.
18.28: Lest the land vomit you out when you make it unclean, as it vomited out the nation that was before you — the direct warning: the same land-vomiting that expelled the Canaanites will expel Israel if they commit the same violations. The symmetry between Canaan's fate and Israel's potential fate is the chapter's most urgent ethical logic; Israel's election does not exempt them from the land's moral ecology. The prophets later invoke this warning as the theological explanation for the Babylonian exile (Ezek 36:17; Amos 2:9-10).
18.29: For everyone who does any of these abominations, the persons who do them shall be cut off from among their people — the comprehensive karet threat for all the prohibitions listed in vv.6-23; the individual penalties specified in Lev 20 (capital vs. karet) refine this general statement, but the basic principle is that any of these violations removes the offender from the covenant community.
18.30: So keep my charge never to practice any of these abominable customs that were practiced before you, and never to make yourselves unclean by them: I am YHWH your God — the final divine self-identification formula closes the entire chapter with the same formula that opened it (v.2). The bracketing ani YHWH Eloheichem encapsulates the chapter's theological argument: all sexual ethics flows from and returns to the covenant identity. The charge to 'keep my charge' (ushmartem et-mishmarti) is the same expression used for the priestly duty (Num 3:28) and for the general covenant obligation (Gen 26:5; Lev 22:9).

19.1: The address shifts from Moses alone to kol adat benei Yisrael — the entire congregation of the people of Israel. This is one of the few times in Leviticus that the audience is the full assembly rather than just Moses or the priests. The Holiness Code (chs. 17-26) addresses all Israel as a priestly people; holiness is not the exclusive concern of the Levitical caste but the calling of the entire covenant community.
19.2: Qedoshim tihyu ki qadosh ani YHWH eloheichem: you shall be holy, for I YHWH your God am holy. The theological foundation of the entire Holiness Code. Holiness is grounded in divine nature, not in human achievement: Israel is to be holy because YHWH is holy. The imitation-of-God ethic (imitatio Dei) that underlies Leviticus 19 — treating others as YHWH treats Israel — is one of the most sophisticated ethical frameworks in the ancient world. The declaration precedes any specific command: the imperative arises from the indicative of divine character.
19.3: Ish imo veavio tirau: each person shall fear (yira) his mother and his father. The reversal of the usual order (father-mother) is deliberate: mother is listed first, perhaps to balance the patriarchal cultural default. Yira (reverence, fear) rather than ahav (love) is used for parents — parental reverence encompasses respect, obedience, and maintenance. The conjunction of parent-fear and Sabbath-keeping in the same verse links domestic ethics and liturgical practice as equally important expressions of holiness.
19.4: Al tifnu el-haelilim: do not turn to idols (elilim = worthless nothings). The prohibition on idols and cast images at the opening of the practical holiness commands mirrors the Decalogue. The Holiness Code presupposes the Decalogue framework: its ethical content is the Decalogue applied across all domains of social life.
19.5: Vechi tizbechu zevach shelamim l-YHWH: when you sacrifice a peace offering (shelamim) to YHWH. The peace offering regulations here (vv. 5-8) parallel those of ch. 7: the offering must be eaten within two days; eating on the third day disqualifies the offering as piggul (pigul = desecration). The time-limit preserves the integrity of the communion meal: the sacred food must be consumed while still fresh, not stored as private provision. The two-day rule makes the peace offering a genuinely communal, time-bounded act of worship.
19.6: Beyom zivchachem ye'achel uvamachorat: it shall be eaten on the day of sacrifice and on the next day. The two-day eating window for the peace offering reflects both practical food-safety concerns (in a pre-refrigeration culture) and theological integrity: the meal is an act of worship, not a stockpile. The parallel in 7:15-18 establishes this as standard Levitical law.
19.7: Ve-im he'achol ye'achel bayom hashlishi piggul hu: if it is eaten at all on the third day, it is piggul (desecrated/loathsome). Piggul (an Akkadian loanword suggesting something offensive or stinking) describes food that has become ritually unfit — not by external contamination but by improper time of consumption. The third-day disqualification renders the entire offering retroactively invalid and the eater bears guilt (avon).
19.8: Veoklav avono yissa ki et-qodesh YHWH chillel: the one who eats it shall bear his iniquity, for he has profaned the holy thing of YHWH. Chillel (to profane, make common) is the opposite of qiddesh (to sanctify, make holy) — the same root as chol (ordinary, secular). Treating sacred food as ordinary by eating it too late collapses the distinction between holy and common that is fundamental to Levitical theology.
19.9: Uvekutzrachem et-qetzir artzechem lo techaleh peat sadecha liqtzor: when you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap to the edge (peat) of your field. The gleaning laws (vv. 9-10) are the Holiness Code's most direct expression of economic ethics. Peat (corner, edge) and laqat (to glean, pick up fallen grain) define the portions intentionally left for the poor. Agricultural abundance comes with a built-in redistribution mechanism: the edges of the field and the dropped grain belong to the poor and the stranger by divine command. Ruth 2 is the narrative embodiment of this law.
19.10: Vecharmelcha lo teolel uferaten karmecha lo telaqqet laani velagen tetov otam: you shall not strip your vineyard bare (teolel = to glean thoroughly, pick over), and you shall not gather the fallen grapes (peret) — leave them for the poor and the sojourner. The vineyard parallel to the grain-field: both have an intentional margin of incompletion. The gleaning laws institutionalize generosity by making it a matter of divine command rather than voluntary charity. The poor have a right to the margins; the owner must actively restrain from full extraction.
19.11: Lo tignovu velo techachedu velo teshaqru ish bamito: you shall not steal (ganav), deal falsely (kachash), or lie to one another. The triplet of theft, deception, and lying covers the main modes of dishonest social interaction. Kachash (to deny, deal falsely, deceive by understatement) and shaqar (to lie) together cover both implicit and explicit dishonesty. The specific construction ish bamito (a man toward his fellow/companion) places these prohibitions in the context of neighbor-relationships — the same context as the love-neighbor command of v18.
19.12: Velo tishavu bishmi lashaqer: you shall not swear falsely by my name. The false oath invokes the divine name to guarantee a lie — a double violation: dishonesty plus misuse of the divine name (cf. the third commandment, Exod 20:7). Challel et shem elohecha (you would profane the name of your God) — false oaths are not merely contractual fraud but theological profanation. The name of YHWH cannot be used as an instrument of deception without damaging the relationship between the community and its God.
19.13: Lo taashoq et-reecha velo tigzol: you shall not oppress (ashaq) your neighbor or rob (gazal) him. Ashaq (to oppress, exploit, extort) covers economic coercion — using power to extract from those who are vulnerable. The wages of a hired worker must not be held overnight — lo-talin peulat sachir itecha ad-boqer. The same day-wage rule appears in Deut 24:15. ANE labor contracts often included provisions about timely payment, but here it is a divine command. The vulnerability of the day laborer — who depends on same-day wages for that evening's food — means delayed payment is effectively theft.
19.14: Lo teqallel cheresh velifnei iver lo titen michshol: you shall not curse the deaf (cheresh), and before the blind (iver) you shall not put a stumbling block. The deaf person cannot hear the curse; the blind cannot see the obstacle placed before them — they are by definition unable to defend themselves. The prohibition of exploiting vulnerability is extended to situations where the victim cannot even perceive the offense. Veyarerta mielohecha: you shall fear (yare) your God — the divine omniscience supplies what human accountability lacks; YHWH sees even what the victim cannot.
19.15: Lo ta'asu avel bamishpat: you shall not do injustice (avel) in judgment (mishpat). The prohibition of partiality in legal proceedings — not favoring the poor (dal) or being partial to the great (gadol). The symmetric prohibition against both economic-class favoritism (favoring the poor against the rich) and power-class favoritism (favoring the wealthy/influential) establishes that justice is blind to social status. Betzedek tishpot amitecha: in righteousness you shall judge your fellow. The positive formulation completes the negative: not just avoiding injustice but actively practicing righteousness.
19.16: Lo telech rachil baamecha: you shall not go about (halak) as a slanderer (rachil) among your people. Rachil (slanderer, gossip, from the same root as merchant/trader) suggests someone who traffics in damaging information about others. Lo taamod al dam reecha: you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor — failing to act when someone's life is at stake. The juxtaposition of slander (active harm through speech) with failure to rescue (passive harm through silence) covers both the misuse and the withholding of speech in neighbor relationships.
19.17: Lo tisna et-achicha bilvavecha: you shall not hate your brother in your heart. The internalization of the ethical command is significant: the prohibition extends beyond external action to interior attitude. Bilvavecha (in your heart) makes hidden contempt as prohibited as open hostility. Hocheach tochiach et-amitecha: you shall certainly rebuke (hocheach tochiach, infinitive absolute for emphasis) your neighbor. Active rebuke is commanded as the alternative to silent resentment — confronting a wrong is more loving than nursing a grievance. Velo tisa alav chet: you shall not bear sin because of him — failing to rebuke makes the bystander complicit in the sin.
19.18: Lo tiqom velo-titor et-benei ammecha veahavta lereiacha kamocha ani YHWH: you shall not take vengeance or bear a grudge (nator = to keep, maintain, preserve — a grudge is a grievance preserved) against the sons of your people, but you shall love (ahav) your neighbor (rea) as yourself. The command to love neighbor is the climax of the preceding sequence (vv. 11-17). Rea (neighbor, fellow, companion) in context covers the full range of social relationships within the community. The self-referential standard (kamocha = as yourself) grounds the command in universal human self-interest: the measure of what you owe your neighbor is what you know you need yourself. The closing ani YHWH (I am YHWH) supplies both authority and motivation: the covenant God who loves Israel commands Israel to extend that love laterally.
19.19: Huqqotai tishmorunu: you shall keep my statutes. The section on forbidden mixtures (kilayim) addresses: (1) breeding livestock with different species; (2) sowing a field with two kinds of seed (sha'atnez applies here); (3) wearing a garment woven from two materials (sha'atnez = mixed fiber cloth, wool-and-linen). The kilayim (mixtures, from kele = to restrain, hold back) prohibitions preserve divinely established categories. The same principle underlying the clean/unclean distinctions — categories must not be collapsed — governs the kilayim rules: the integrity of created kinds reflects the integrity of the Creator's order.
19.20: Ve-ish ki yishkav et-isha shikhvat-zera: when a man lies with a woman and there is intercourse. This case involves a slave woman betrothed to another man but not yet redeemed or granted freedom. The case sits between fully free and fully slave, creating a liminal legal status that requires a specific ruling. Because she is not fully free, the death penalty for adultery does not apply; instead a biqqoret (investigation, inquiry, or scourging — the term is debated) is required, and a guilt offering (asham) is brought.
19.21: Vehevi et-ashamo l-YHWH: he shall bring his guilt offering (asham) to YHWH. The guilt offering in this case provides atonement for the violation of the betrothed slave woman's protected status. The requirement of a formal atonement ritual for what might otherwise be treated as a purely civil matter demonstrates that sexual ethics in the Holiness Code has a theological dimension: violations against persons are violations against YHWH's covenant order.
19.22: Vechipper alav hakkohen biil ha-asham lifnei YHWH al-chatato asher chata venislach lo me-chatato asher chata: the priest shall make atonement for him before YHWH, and he shall be forgiven for the sin he has committed. The atonement-and-forgiveness formula here — identical to the standard sin/guilt offering formula — applies even in the slave-woman case. No violation is beyond the reach of the atonement system, but none is beneath requiring it either.
19.23: Veki tavo-u el-haaretz untemtem kol-etz maakal vaarltem orlato et-piryo: when you enter the land and plant any fruit tree, you shall treat its fruit as forbidden (orlah, literally: uncircumcised). The first three years of fruit are arel (uncircumcised, forbidden) — not to be eaten. Orlah (the foreskin; used metaphorically for what is sealed/restricted) is applied to fruit, connecting the circumcision covenant-mark to agricultural practice. The first-fruits theology requires patient waiting: the tree must establish itself before humans take its fruit. Mishnah Orlah is an entire tractate devoted to the practical application of this law.
19.24: Uvhashanah hareviyit yihyeh kol-piryo qodesh hillulim l-YHWH: in the fourth year all its fruit shall be holy — a jubilation offering (hillulim, from halel = to praise) to YHWH. The fourth-year fruit is entirely dedicated to YHWH as a praise offering — hillulim is a festive, joyful offering. Only in the fifth year may the owner eat the fruit for personal benefit. The delayed-gratification structure (3 years forbidden + 1 year dedicated + 5th year yours) teaches that the land's produce belongs to YHWH first.
19.25: Uvhashanah hachamishit tochelu et-piryo lehosif lachem tevuatah: in the fifth year you may eat its fruit, to increase its yield for you — I am YHWH your God. The obedience to the orlah laws increases the tree's yield (lehosif... tevuatah): the theological principle that covenant obedience produces abundance is applied even to horticultural practice. The closing ani YHWH eloheichem connects the agricultural law to covenant identity.
19.26: Lo tochelu al-hadam: you shall not eat any flesh with the blood (al-hadam). The prohibition on blood-eating echoes Lev 17:10-14 and connects to the ANE divination practice of eating flesh with blood in magical/cultic contexts. The following prohibitions on divination (nachash) and augury (anan) are in the same cluster — the blood-eating, divination, and augury prohibitions address practices that sought to access spiritual power outside the covenant framework.
19.27: Lo taqifu peat roshechem velo tashchit et-peat zeqanecha: you shall not round off the hair on your temples (peat rosh) or mar the edges of your beard. The hair-cutting prohibition addresses mourning practices common in Canaanite religion — shaving specific areas of the head and beard was associated with pagan rituals for the dead. The prohibition distinguishes Israelite mourning from syncretistic practices (cf. Deut 14:1). The peat rosh regulations may also be connected to clan-identification markings.
19.28: Veseret lanefesh lo titenu bivesarchem uchtovt qaqqa lo titenu bachem: you shall not make any cuts (seret) on your body for the dead, or tattoo yourselves (ketovot qa'aqa) — I am YHWH. Both cutting for the dead (a mourning rite common in Baal worship; cf. 1 Kgs 18:28) and tattooing (permanent body marking) are prohibited as pagan practices that mark the body as belonging to another deity or that participate in death-cult rituals. Ani YHWH closes the prohibition: the body belongs to YHWH, not to the death-powers of surrounding religion.
19.29: Al techallel et-bitcha lehaznotah: do not degrade your daughter by making her a prostitute (zanah). The warning against cult prostitution (or economic exploitation of daughters through prostitution) — velo-tizne haaretz umalah haaretz zimmah (lest the land become full of zimmah, a term for sexual wickedness/shameful scheming). The land itself is conceived as a moral participant: sexual sin fills the land with corruption in the same way that blood guilt does (Num 35:33).
19.30: Et-shabbetotai tishmoru umiqdashi tirau: you shall keep my Sabbaths and reverence my sanctuary — the refrain that brackets the Holiness Code (cf. 26:2). The Sabbath and sanctuary commands appear again here in the middle of the chapter's ethical content, reminding that the social ethics of ch. 19 cannot be separated from the liturgical framework: ethical behavior and worship belong together.
19.31: Al tifnu el-haoboth veal haydeonim al-tevaqshu letomah bahem: do not turn to mediums (ovoth) or wizards/necromancers (yideoni). Ov (a medium, one who consults the dead — possibly referring to the pit-spirit through which necromancers communicated) and yideoni (a knowing one, a familiar spirit) cover the main forms of occult consultation in the ANE. The prohibition is grounded positively in covenant relationship: seeking knowledge from the dead is a rejection of YHWH as the source of guidance and knowledge.
19.32: Mipnei seivah taqum vehadarta penei zaqen veyarata meelohecha: you shall stand (taqum) before the gray-haired and honor the face of the aged, and you shall fear your God. The elderly are owed honor not because of individual achievement but because of age itself — a principle that cuts against both meritocracy and youth-culture. The connection between honoring elders and fearing God (veyarata meelohecha) grounds intergenerational respect in the same theological place as respect for the vulnerable: YHWH sees what human status-systems ignore.
19.33: Vechi yagur itecha ger beartzechem lo tonub: when a stranger (ger) sojourns with you in your land, you shall not wrong (onah) him. Ger (sojourner, resident alien — not a temporary visitor but a long-term resident without native-born rights) receives specific legal protection in the Holiness Code. Onah (to wrong, oppress, afflict — the same root as ana in 19:13) covers economic, social, and legal exploitation of resident foreigners.
19.34: Keozerach mikem yihyeh lachem hager hagor itchem veahavta lo kamocha ki gerim heyitem beeretz mitzraim: the stranger who sojourns with you shall be to you as the native among you, and you shall love him as yourself, for you were strangers in the land of Egypt. The extension of the love-neighbor command (v18) to the ger is among the most remarkable commands in Leviticus. The motivation is empathetic-historical: Israel's own experience as foreigners (gerim) in Egypt is the ground for treating foreigners well. The Exodus memory is the ethical engine that drives neighbor-extension.
19.35: Lo ta'asu avel bamishpat bammidah bammishqal uvammeshura: you shall not do injustice in judgment, in measures of length (midah), in weight (mishqal), or in quantity (meshurah). The prohibition on false weights and measures covers three dimensions of commercial measurement. The ANE merchant carried two sets of weights — heavier for buying, lighter for selling — a practice explicitly condemned here and in Amos 8:5, Micah 6:11, Proverbs 11:1.
19.36: Mozanei tzedeq avnei tzedeq eifat tzedeq vehin tzedeq yihyeh lachem: just balances (mozanei tzedeq), just weights (avnei tzedeq), a just ephah (eifat tzedeq), and a just hin (hin tzedeq) you shall have. Tzedeq (righteousness, justice, right) applied to commercial instruments: the scales, stones, and measures must themselves be characterized by justice. The context is the Exodus: ani YHWH eloheichem asher hotzeiti etchem meeretz mitzraim (I am YHWH your God who brought you out of Egypt) — the God who redeemed Israel with exact precision demands exact precision in commercial dealings.
19.37: Ushmartem et kol-chuqotai veet-kol-mishpatai vaasitem otam ani YHWH: you shall observe all my statutes and all my rules and do them — I am YHWH. The closing verse of the Holiness Code's practical ethics section provides the same double formula (keep + do) that opens ch. 19 (v3: tishmoru... veshabbetotai). The bracket of Sabbath/sanctuary at vv. 3 and 30, combined with the opening (v2) and closing (v37) holiness-formula, gives ch. 19 a carefully structured literary shape around the central love-neighbor command of v18.

20.1: The penalty chapter (ch. 20) is the enforcement counterpart to the prohibition chapter (ch. 18). Where ch. 18 lists the forbidden sexual relations and practices, ch. 20 assigns specific penalties. The chapter is addressed again to benei Yisrael — the full community, not just the priests.
20.2: Ish ish mibenei Yisrael umin-hager hagar beYisrael asher yiten mizaro laMolech mot yumat: any Israelite or sojourner in Israel who gives his children to Molech (laMolech) shall be put to death. Molech is a deity associated with child sacrifice — the name may derive from melek (king) or from the offering term molech/mulk (a type of vow-sacrifice in Punic inscriptions). The tofet (furnace/hearth) in the Hinnom Valley outside Jerusalem (Jer 7:31) was the site of Molech-child sacrifice. The stoning penalty for Molech worship is carried out by am haaretz (the people of the land) — communal enforcement rather than judicial execution alone.
20.3: Va-ani etten et-panai ba-ish hahu vehichratti oto miqqerev ammo: and I will set my face against that man and cut him off (karet) from among his people. The divine face-setting against Molech worshipers introduces both human penalty (stoning) and divine penalty (karet = being cut off). Karet (cutting off) is the distinctive Levitical divine penalty — distinct from human-executed punishment, it describes premature death or exclusion from covenant community at the divine level. Giving children to Molech is specifically described as defiling YHWH's sanctuary and profaning his holy name — the theological gravity matches the penalty's severity.
20.4: Ve-im haalem yaaleimu am haaretz et-eineihem min-ha-ish hahu: if the people of the land at all close their eyes (haalem yaaleimu, an emphatic infinitive absolute) to that man. Social complicity — willful ignorance of a covenant violation — is itself punishable. The community that fails to act against Molech worship shares in its guilt. The communal enforcement mechanism built into the Holiness Code makes every Israelite a stakeholder in covenant maintenance.
20.5: Vesamti ani et-panai ba-ish hahu uvemishpachto vehichratti oto: I myself will set my face against that man and his family and cut him off. The divine penalty extends to the family — not because relatives are automatically guilty but because participation in Molech worship was typically a family affair, and the community of the household shares in its head's apostasy.
20.6: Vehanefesh asher tifneh el-haoboth veel-hayideonim liznot achareihem: the person who turns to mediums (ovoth) and necromancers (yideonim) to prostitute himself by following them. The language of spiritual prostitution (liznot) connects occult consultation with idolatry — both are covenant infidelity. The penalty is karet — divine cutting off.
20.7: Vehitqaddashtem vihyitem qedoshim ki ani YHWH eloheichem: consecrate yourselves and be holy, for I am YHWH your God. The holiness refrain (cf. 19:2) appears here at the center of the penalty chapter, reminding that the penalties serve a constructive purpose: they protect the holiness of the community. The penalties are not merely punitive but purgative — removing the corruption that threatens the integrity of the covenant people.
20.8: Ushmartem et-chuqqotai vaasitem otam ani YHWH meqaddishchem: keep my statutes and do them — I am YHWH who sanctifies you (meqaddishchem). YHWH is the agent of Israel's sanctification: Israel does not make itself holy through observance but is made holy by YHWH. The keeping of statutes is the human response to divine sanctification, not its cause.
20.9: Ki-ish ish asher yeqallel et-aviv veet-immo mot yumat: anyone who curses his father or mother shall be put to death. The capital penalty for cursing parents places parental honor at the level of covenant seriousness — consistent with the fifth commandment's position in the Decalogue. Aviv veimmo qillel: he has cursed his father and his mother — his blood is upon him (damo bo). The self-responsibility formula (damo bo) recurs throughout the penalty chapter, indicating that the condemned person bears the moral weight of their own execution.
20.10: Veish asher yinaf et-eshet ish asher yinaf et-eshet reehub: a man who commits adultery with the wife of his fellow. Both the adulterer and the adulteress shall be put to death. The symmetry of the penalty — both parties equally liable — reflects a significant level of gender equity in the judicial framework compared to some ANE law codes that punished only the woman. The equal penalty affirms that both parties acted as moral agents and both bear equal responsibility.
20.11: Veish asher yishkav et-eshet aviv: the man who lies with his father's wife. This covers both the man and the woman: both shall be put to death, their blood is upon them. The penalty section (vv. 10-21) applies capital punishment or karet to the forbidden relations listed in ch. 18, working through the same categories: adultery (v10), father's wife (v11), daughter-in-law (v12), male homosexual intercourse (v13), and so on.
20.12: Veish asher yishkav et-khalato: a man who lies with his daughter-in-law — both shall be put to death. Tevel (confusion, perversion) is the term for this violation — the same word used in Lev 18:23 for bestiality. The term reflects the Levitical categorization: these acts mix what should be kept distinct (parent/child-in-law roles; human/animal categories).
20.13: Ve-ish asher yishkav et-zachar mishkevei isha: a man who lies with a male as with a woman — both have committed an abomination (to'evah). The same term (to'evah) and the same capital penalty as in 18:22. The context of the penalty chapter's systematic treatment of all forbidden relations shows this is not a marginal concern in the Holiness Code but is treated as seriously as adultery and incest.
20.14: Veish asher yiqqach et-isha veet-imma zimmah hi: a man who takes a woman and her mother — it is depravity (zimmah). All three shall be burned (basesh yisrefu) — the distinctive penalty of burning (rather than stoning) is reserved for the most egregious cases (cf. Lev 21:9 for a priest's daughter). The severity of the penalty reflects the severity of the violation: multiple kinship bonds collapsed simultaneously.
20.15: Veish asher yitten shekhavto bivehemah: a man who lies with an animal shall be put to death, and you shall kill the animal as well. The animal's execution (despite its lack of moral agency) serves to remove the instrument of the violation from the community — a practice paralleled in the case of a goring ox (Exod 21:28-29). The animal is a davar nevelah (something unclean); its removal prevents any further use of it that would perpetuate the defilement.
20.16: Ve-isha asher tiqrav el kol-behemah lerivatah otah: and a woman who approaches any animal to lie with it — you shall kill the woman and the animal. The equal treatment of male and female in the bestiality penalties is consistent with the chapter's pattern of bilateral moral accountability.
20.17: Veish asher yiqqach et-achoto bat-aviv o vat-immo: a man who takes his sister — daughter of his father or daughter of his mother. The penalty is karet rather than execution, suggesting a different level of severity even within the category of forbidden unions. Chesed hu (it is a disgrace/shame) — chesed in this unusual sense means shameful lovingkindness (the word's positive meaning inverted) — some render it reproach or disgrace.
20.18: Veish asher yishkav et-isha davah: a man who lies with a woman during her menstrual impurity — both shall be cut off (nikretu) from among their people. The mutual karet penalty for deliberate violation of the niddah restriction is lighter than execution but still serious. The bilateral penalty again emphasizes mutual moral agency.
20.19: Veervet achat imecha veervet achat avicha lo tegalleh: you shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother's sister or your father's sister — it would uncover one's own flesh (sheer). The karet penalty applies. The aunt relationships involve not just the direct kin but the extension of the kin-network into which one is embedded.
20.20: Veish asher yishkav et-dodato: a man who lies with his uncle's wife. This case does not receive the execution penalty but instead: chataam yis'u (they shall bear their punishment/sin), and they shall die childless (aririn yamuto). The death-without-children penalty is understood as a divine curse rather than a judicial execution — a form of the Holiness Code's awareness that covenant violations bring natural consequences, not only judicial ones.
20.21: Veish asher yiqqach et-eshet achiv niddah hi: a man who takes his brother's wife — it is impurity (niddah). The same consequence: they shall die childless. This is the reverse of the levirate marriage institution (Deut 25:5-10) where marrying a deceased brother's wife was required. The different treatment reflects whether the brother is alive (here: forbidden, treated as adultery) or dead without children (Deut 25: commanded to preserve the family name).
20.22: Ushmartem et-kol-chuqqotai veet-kol-mishpatai vaasitem otam: keep all my statutes and all my rules and do them. The land-vomiting warning reappears: velo taqii etchem haaretz asher ani meivi etchem shamah lashevet ba (so that the land to which I am bringing you to dwell there does not vomit you out). The land is personified as a moral participant that cannot tolerate persistent covenant violation — the same imagery as 18:28.
20.23: Velo telechu bechukot hagoy asher anochi meshalleach mipeneichem: you shall not walk in the statutes of the nation that I am sending out before you. The Canaanites' expulsion from the land is attributed to their practice of the very offenses listed in the chapter — not ethnic inferiority but covenant-violation resulted in their removal. This theology prevents Israel from claiming special treatment: the same standard applies to them if they commit the same violations.
20.24: Va-omar lachem atem tirbu et-admatam va-ani atenah lachem la-reshet otah eretz zavat chalav udevash: I said to you that you would inherit their land — a land flowing with milk and honey. The promise of the land is grounded in the distinction YHWH made between Israel and the nations: va-avdil etchem min-haamim lihyot li (I have separated you from the peoples to be mine). The separation theology of the Holiness Code serves an election purpose: Israel's distinctiveness is not superiority but vocation — to be YHWH's covenant people.
20.25: Vahivdaltem bein habehemah hatehorah letemeah: you shall therefore separate (havdil) the clean from the unclean beast and between unclean and clean birds. The havdalah (separation, distinction) that structures the purity laws is the same cognitive and ethical act demanded in all of Leviticus: maintaining the distinctions that YHWH established at creation. Velo tesha'qetzu et-nafshoteichem: you shall not defile yourselves through the animals. The connection between dietary separation and holiness-separation is explicit here.
20.26: Vihyitem li qedoshim ki qadosh ani YHWH veavdil etchem min-haamim lihyot li: you shall be holy to me, for I YHWH am holy, and I have separated you from the peoples to be mine. The holiness refrain reaches its fullest expression here: the source of the command (YHWH's holiness), the content (separation from the nations), and the purpose (to belong to YHWH) are all stated together. The closing verse of the penalty chapter's theological section presents holiness as both inherited (YHWH's character) and achieved (Israel's calling).
20.27: Ve-ish o isha ki yihyeh bahem ov o yideoni mot yumatu: a man or woman who is a medium (ov) or wizard (yideoni) shall be put to death — stoned, their blood is upon them. The chapter ends with the execution penalty for the two main forms of occult practice (cf. 19:31), bracketing the chapter with the theme of unauthorized access to spiritual knowledge. The penalties for Molech worship (ch. 20 opening) and occult practice (ch. 20 closing) frame all the sexual and kinship prohibitions between them, suggesting that all three — child sacrifice, sexual violations, and necromancy — are expressions of the same fundamental rejection of YHWH's covenant order.

21.1: Chapter 21 addresses priestly holiness specifically — the higher standard required of those who serve at the altar and approach the divine presence. El-hakkohanim benei Aharon: to the priests, the sons of Aaron. The priesthood has additional restrictions beyond those of ordinary Israelites, proportional to their proximity to the holy. The principle is consistent throughout Leviticus: the closer to the divine presence, the stricter the purity requirements.
21.2: Ki im-lisheero haqarov alav: except for his nearest kin — mother, father, son, daughter, brother. Priests may defile themselves (yitamma = become ritually impure) for these immediate family members by handling their corpses. The permitted exceptions define the irreducible family circle within which even priestly purity requirements yield to the obligations of family love and burial duty. The restriction to immediate relatives makes the list of exceptions minimal and precise.
21.3: Ulechistoh habetulah haqerovah elaiv asher lo hayta leish lah yittamma: for his sister, a virgin who is near to him and has had no husband, he may become unclean. The unmarried sister (still in the paternal household, not transferred to another family unit) counts among the immediate family for whom a priest may defile himself. The married sister, having joined another household, is no longer counted — a reflection of the patrilineal household structure where marriage transferred a woman's primary familial identity.
21.4: Lo yitamma ba'al beammav lechallelo: he shall not defile himself as a husband among his people, so as to profane himself. This verse is much disputed — it may refer to an in-law relationship or to a general prohibition against the priest defiling himself for more distant kin beyond the immediate family list.
21.5: Lo yiqrechu qorcha beroshom ufe'at zeqanam lo yegallechu: they shall not make bald patches on their heads or shave off the edges of their beards or make cuts (seret) on their flesh. The three prohibited mourning practices (head-shaving, beard-trimming, cutting the body) that were prohibited for all Israelites in 19:27-28 are here specifically prohibited for priests. The priest's body must remain unmarked — whole and undamaged — as befits one who represents the wholeness of the covenant community before YHWH.
21.6: Qedoshim yihyu leiloheichem velo yechalelu shem eloheihem: they shall be holy to their God and not profane the name of their God. The priest's holiness is specifically connected to the divine name: the priest's actions directly reflect on YHWH's name in a way that ordinary Israelites' actions do not. The priest mediates between the community and God — any defilement of the priest is a profanation of the divine name because the priest represents YHWH to the people and the people to YHWH.
21.7: Isha zonah vachalala lo yiqachu: they shall not marry a prostitute (zonah) or a woman who has been defiled (chalala = profaned/desecrated, a woman who has lost her ritual-purity status) or a woman divorced from her husband. The marriage restrictions for priests reflect the requirement that the priestly household be free from anything that carries the connotations of defilement or social dishonor. Chalala (the profaned/desecrated woman) is the feminine form of chillel (to profane) — a woman excluded from priestly circles.
21.8: Veqiddashto ki et-lechem elohecha hu maqriv: you shall sanctify (qiddashto) him, for he offers the bread of your God. The entire community has a stake in priestly holiness: veqiddashto is addressed to Israel collectively. The priestly holiness belongs not to the individual priest alone but to the community that depends on his mediation. YHWH himself is the sanctifier: ki qadosh ani YHWH meqaddishchem (for holy am I, YHWH who sanctifies you).
21.9: Uvat ish kohen ki techel liznot: and the daughter of a priest, if she profanes herself by whoring (liznot). She shall be burned with fire (va-esh tisref) — the most severe penalty in the chapter. The priest's daughter represents the priestly household's honor in a way that ordinary Israelites' daughters do not. Her sexual misconduct profanes her father: et-aviha hi mechalelet. The burning penalty (also in 20:14) is reserved for the most serious cases in the Holiness Code.
21.10: Vehakohen haggadol me-echav asher-yutzaq al-rosho shemen hamishcha: the high priest (kohen haggadol, literally the great priest) — upon whose head the anointing oil (shemen hamishcha) has been poured and who has been consecrated to wear the garments. The high priest bears the highest standard of holiness. He shall not uncover his head (lo yifra rosho) or tear his garments (bigadav lo yifrom) — the two standard mourning actions that even ordinary priests are restricted in performing. The high priest is completely prohibited from both.
21.11: Veal kol-nafshot met lo yavo: he shall not go near any dead body at all. The high priest may not defile himself even for his own father or mother — the strictest possible application of the proximity-holiness principle. The high priest's ongoing ritual purity is so essential to the functioning of the sanctuary (especially on Yom Kippur) that personal bereavement cannot be allowed to disqualify him. This creates an extraordinary human cost: the high priest must refrain from burying his own parents.
21.12: Umin-hamiqddash lo yetze velo yechalel et-miqdash elohav: he shall not leave the sanctuary (hamiqddash) or profane the sanctuary of his God. The high priest may not leave to attend a funeral even outside the sanctuary precincts. The reason: ki nezer shemen mishchat elohav alav (for the consecration of the anointing oil of his God is upon him). Nezer (consecration, crown — also the crown/diadem of the high priest's turban, Lev 8:9) describes the high priest's permanent state of consecration: the anointing oil that marks him as set apart cannot be temporarily set aside for personal mourning.
21.13: Vehu isha bivtuleiha yiqqach: he shall take a wife in her virginity (bivtuleiha). The high priest's wife must be a virgin (betulah) — an additional restriction beyond the ordinary priest's marriage rules (v7). The requirement of a virgin wife for the high priest reflects the highest level of the purity-and-wholeness principle: the high priest's household must be free from any previous sexual relationship that might carry the associations of mixed loyalty or prior covenant.
21.14: Almana ugerushah vechalala zonah et-eleh lo yiqqach: a widow, a divorced woman, a desecrated woman, a prostitute — these he shall not marry. The list for the high priest is more extensive than for ordinary priests (v7): widows are also excluded (ordinary priests may marry widows). Only betulat amav (a virgin from his own people) is permitted. The restriction to a virgin from his own people preserves the high priest's household as a symbol of unbroken covenant fidelity.
21.15: Velo yechalel zaro beammav: he shall not profane his offspring among his people. The high priest's marriage restrictions are motivated by dynastic as well as personal holiness: the priestly line must be maintained in an unambiguous state of holiness. Ki ani YHWH meqaddisho: for I am YHWH who sanctifies him — YHWH himself is the source of the high priest's sanctification, which the marriage rules protect and maintain.
21.16: YHWH speaks to Moses to deliver the physical-defect regulations for priests who cannot offer the bread of God at the altar.
21.17: Ish min-zaracha ledorotam asher yihyeh vo mum lo yiqrav lehaqriv lechem elohav: any man of Aaron's descendants throughout their generations who has a defect (mum) may not approach to offer the bread of his God. Mum (defect, blemish, spot) is the same word used for the unblemished quality required of sacrificial animals. The principle of correspondence: as the offering must be without defect, so must the offerer. The priest represents the perfection of what human beings are called to be before God — physical wholeness symbolizes the spiritual and moral integrity required for covenant mediation.
21.18: The list of disqualifying defects: iver (blind), pisseach (lame), charum (mutilated face/nose — or with a defect of the nose, debated), sarua (a limb too long), shever regel (broken foot), shever yad (broken hand).
21.19: Gibbein o daq: hunchbacked (gibbein) or dwarfed (daq). The list continues with conditions affecting posture and stature. Tevalul beeino: defect in his eye — perhaps a cataract or other visual impairment beyond full blindness. Garav: itch/scurvy. Yalefet: scabs. Merua ashech: crushed testicle — the final item in the list covers damage to the reproductive system.
21.20: This verse continues listing the defects (including crushed testicle) from v19. The comprehensive list demonstrates that the restriction covers all categories of physical imperfection — not as a moral judgment but as a symbolic system: those who represent the covenant community before a holy God must embody the completeness and wholeness that the covenant life is designed to produce.
21.21: Kol-ish asher-bo mum mizera Aharon hakohen lo yiggash lehaqriv et-ishei YHWH: any man of Aaron's descendants who has a defect shall not approach to offer the fire offerings of YHWH. The prohibiton is absolute for altar service, but qualified in the next verse: the defective priest may still eat the sacred food.
21.22: Lechem elohav miqodshei haqodashim umin-haqodashim yochal: he may eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy things and of the holy things. The defective priest retains his status as priest and his right to eat the sacred food; he is only prohibited from active altar service. The distinction preserves his dignity and his covenant standing while maintaining the integrity of the altar service. The defect affects what he may do, not who he is.
21.23: Ach el-happarochet lo yavo ve-el-hamizbeach lo yiggash: but he shall not go through the veil (parochet) or approach the altar. The two most sacred locations — the veil separating the holy of holies and the altar of burnt offering — are specifically prohibited to the defective priest. His access to the holiest spaces is restricted even though his priesthood continues. The spatial restriction mirrors the graduated holiness structure of the entire sanctuary complex.
21.24: Vayedaber Moshe el-Aharon veel-banav veel-kol-benei Yisrael: Moses spoke to Aaron and to his sons and to all the people of Israel. The priestly holiness laws of ch. 21 are communicated to the whole people — not kept as arcane priestly knowledge but made public. The community's understanding of priestly standards matters because the community depends on and has a stake in the integrity of its mediators. The chapter closes with the widest possible audience for the priestly holiness code.

22.1: Chapter 22 continues the regulations for the Aaronic priesthood begun in chapter 21. Where chapter 21 addressed the personal holiness requirements of priests (physical wholeness, marriage rules, mourning prohibitions), chapter 22 addresses functional holiness: when priests may eat the sacred donations and what quality of animal is acceptable for sacrifice. The chapter belongs to the Holiness Code (chs. 17-26), which repeatedly grounds Israel's distinctiveness in YHWH's own holiness ('I am YHWH who sanctifies you,' v.32). The priestly regulations here are the internal counterpart to the general Israelite food and purity laws of chapters 11-15.
22.2: The command that priests 'keep away from the holy things' when impure protects the integrity of the sacred donations (qodashim). The priestly house received portions of the offerings — the terumah (heave offering), the breast and right thigh of peace offerings, the unleavened bread from Nazirite offerings, and portions of grain offerings. These donations sustained the priestly family economically in exchange for their tabernacle service. The system of sacred donations was also a form of social security for the priestly tribe, which owned no land (Num 18:20-24). Malachi 1:6-14 shows how the system was abused when priests accepted inferior animals and treated the sacred donations with contempt.
22.3: The karet (cutting off) penalty for eating holy food while ritually impure appears here as in other crucial holiness violations. In context, the priest who eats sacred food while impure has treated the holy as common — this is precisely the profanation (chillul) that the Holiness Code most consistently condemns. Second Temple priestly practice included elaborate procedures for confirming ritual purity before eating terumah: handwashing, immersion, and waiting periods were all part of maintaining the system the Torah requires.
22.4: The specific impurity conditions that disqualify a priest from eating sacred donations — skin disease (tzara'at), genital discharge (zob), corpse-contamination — are the same major categories addressed in chapters 13-15. The cross-referential structure is deliberate: the purity-law block and the priestly-regulation block are designed to be read together. A reader who has absorbed chapters 11-15 already knows exactly what conditions the priest must avoid and how to resolve each one.
22.5: Contact impurity transmission: touching someone who transmits corpse-impurity, or a person with a discharge, makes the priest impure. The impurity-chain logic (person A touched corpse → person B touched A → person B is now impure) works through physical contact. Numbers 19 develops the corpse-impurity rules in detail with the red heifer purification. The Second Temple period saw vigorous debate about the specifics of impurity transmission — the Pharisees and Sadducees disagreed on many points of application.
22.6: 'The person who touches any such thing shall be unclean until the evening and shall not eat of the holy things unless he has bathed his body in water.' The resolution mechanism for most minor impurities — water immersion + evening — was the standard baseline. Later Jewish practice formalized this as the mikveh (ritual immersion pool), which became a standard installation in any Jewish community. The evening boundary (when a new day begins in Jewish reckoning) created a clean daily reset for ordinary impurities.
22.7: 'When the sun goes down he shall be clean, and afterward he may eat of the holy things, for they are his food.' The priest's access to sacred donations is not a spiritual luxury but his sustenance — 'for they are his food.' The Levitical system created a labor-for-donations economy: priests served at the altar and in turn received portions of the offerings. Paul cites this principle directly: 'those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel' (1 Cor 9:13-14), explicitly drawing on the Levitical pattern.
22.8: Nevela (animal that died naturally) and trefah (animal torn by predators) are prohibited to priests — a stricter standard than for ordinary Israelites, who could give such meat to foreigners (Deut 14:21). The priestly diet was a subset of the broader Israelite dietary laws: what was permissible for the laity was not necessarily permissible for priests. This graduated holiness — ordinary Israelites, Levites, priests, high priest — each with progressively stricter purity requirements — reflects the concentric structure of the tabernacle itself (courts, holy place, holy of holies).
22.9: 'They shall therefore keep my charge, lest they bear sin for it and die thereby when they profane it.' The death penalty for priestly mishandling of holy things is not merely threatened but enacted in the narrative: Nadab and Abihu died for offering unauthorized fire (Lev 10:1-2); Uzzah died for touching the ark improperly (2 Sam 6:7). These narratives function as cautionary examples that make the abstract warning of v.9 concrete. The priestly vocation was both privilege and mortal risk.
22.10: The household boundary for eating terumah: zar (stranger/outsider) cannot eat it. The 'stranger' here is not necessarily a non-Israelite but anyone not part of the priestly household by birth or purchase. A resident worker (toshav) or hired laborer (sakhir) employed by the priest — who lives in the house but is not a member of the family — cannot eat the sacred donations. The priestly household is a religious unit with economic boundaries as well as kinship ones.
22.11: A slave purchased by a priest becomes a full member of the priestly household and may eat sacred donations; a slave born in the priest's household has the same status. This is a significant statement: the purchased slave's belonging to the priestly family overrides his non-priestly origin. The principle that membership matters more than birth is embedded in the Levitical system. It contrasts with the daughter who married a non-priest (v.12) — marriage out of the priestly household removes access, while purchase into it grants access.
22.12: A priest's daughter who marries a non-priest (ish zar) loses access to the sacred donations. Her status follows her husband's household — she has become part of a non-priestly economic unit. This rule reflects the ancient Israelite household structure where a woman's status was determined by her husband's household. The loss of terumah access was a real economic consequence of marriage outside the priestly family, not merely a ritual technicality.
22.13: The divorced or widowed daughter who returns to her father's house without children — having no living descendants from the non-priestly marriage — regains access to sacred donations. The condition 'with no children' is key: if she has children, they belong to the non-priestly line and she is presumed to be supporting them, not fully re-integrated into the priestly household. This nuanced rule shows careful attention to the actual family economics of ancient Israel.
22.14: The accidental eating of sacred donations by a layman requires restoration at full value plus a 20% surcharge (chamishit). This parallels the guilt offering regulations (Lev 5:14-16): trespass against the holy — even unintentional — requires real restitution. The 20% premium marks the special status of what was encroached upon. Second Temple halakha developed detailed rules about when and how laypersons might accidentally encounter terumah and their corresponding obligations.
22.15: 'The priests shall not profane the holy things of the people of Israel, which they contribute to YHWH.' The priests bear responsibility not only for their own holiness but for protecting the integrity of the donation system on behalf of the whole people. If priests accept and eat donations improperly, they have profaned what the whole congregation brought to YHWH. This corporate responsibility of the priesthood is part of why the prophets reserved their sharpest critique for priestly corruption (Jer 2:8; Ezek 22:26; Mal 1:6-14).
22.16: 'Causing them to bear iniquity and guilt, by eating their holy things' — the priest's profanation becomes the people's guilt. The sacrificial system was a system of covenant mediation: when the mediating priests corrupted the system, the whole covenant community suffered the consequences. The prophetic warnings about priestly failure (especially Malachi) are grounded in this principle: Israel's relationship with YHWH is damaged by priestly mishandling of its sacred institutions.
22.17: The transition to offering regulations (vv.17-25) addresses the quality of animals brought for burnt offerings and votive offerings. The neder (vow) and nedavah (freewill offering) represent two motivations for additional offerings beyond the required sacrifices: obligation fulfilled and spontaneous generosity. The tamim (perfect/without blemish) requirement applies to both — YHWH does not accept second-tier offerings regardless of the motivation behind them.
22.18: The law addresses offerings from 'the house of Israel or the sojourners in Israel' — resident non-Israelites could also bring offerings to YHWH's altar. This reflects the inclusive dimension of the tabernacle/temple as a place of access for all who aligned themselves with Israel's God. Numbers 15:15-16 makes this explicit: 'one statute for you and the stranger who sojourns among you.' The requirement that alien offerings also be tamim confirms that the standard transcends ethnic category.
22.19: 'For your offering to be accepted it shall be a male without blemish of the cattle or the sheep or the goats.' The three livestock categories — cattle, sheep, goats — comprise the whole of the Israelite domesticated herd. The male requirement for burnt and votive offerings (females were permitted for some sin offerings, Lev 4:28, 32) reflected both the economic logic of herd management and the symbolic association of the male with strength and completeness.
22.20: 'Anything that has a blemish, you shall not offer, for it will not be acceptable for you.' The principle of acceptability (ratzon) is the governing criterion: YHWH's pleasure is not a given but depends on the integrity of the offering. Malachi 1:8 applies this directly: 'When you offer blind animals in sacrifice, is that not evil? And when you offer those that are lame or sick, is that not evil? Present that to your governor; will he accept you or show you favor?' The prophetic critique translates the Levitical standard into political-relational terms.
22.21: The peace offering (shelamim) for a vow or freewill offering must be tamim with no blemish. The shelamim's communal character — portions to YHWH, priests, and offerer — meant that a blemished animal would have been consumed by the priestly family. The law protects the integrity of what the priests receive as well as what YHWH 'receives' (consumes in the altar fire). The whole offering economy depended on the tamim standard being maintained at every point.
22.22: The catalog of disqualifying conditions — blind, injured, maimed, with running sores, scabs, or scurvy — covers visible physical defects. The comprehensiveness of the list is designed to close any possible loophole for priestly discretion in accepting marginal animals. Compare with the Second Temple practice: the Mishnah (tractate Bekhorot) devotes extensive chapters to exactly which physical conditions constitute blemishes and which are within normal variation. The Levitical list generated centuries of legal interpretation.
22.23: A freewill offering (nedavah) may be an ox or sheep with a limb too long or too short — a minor asymmetry that would disqualify for vowed offerings but is acceptable for the spontaneous offering.
