25.6: The sabbath produce is for eating — owner, male servant, female servant, hired worker, sojourner, cattle, and wild animals (vv.6-7) all share equally in the seventh-year bounty. This is a remarkable social leveling: the produce that grows freely is not the owner’s private property to sell. The same logic underlies gleaning laws (Lev 19:9-10; 23:22): the margins of the harvest belong to the poor, widow, orphan, and stranger. The shemittah takes this principle to its maximal expression for one whole year.
25.7: Wild animals (hayyat ha-aretz) eat from the seventh-year growth. Including wild animals in the beneficiary list is ecologically and theologically significant: the land’s rest benefits non-human creation. This resonates with the sabbath commandment in Ex 20:10 (livestock rest too) and Deut 25:4 (do not muzzle the ox). The ANE conception of land as a resource for human exploitation is qualified in Israel by YHWH’s ownership claim and the rights of non-human creation.
25.8: “You shall count seven weeks of years — seven times seven years — so that the seven weeks of years give you forty-nine years.” The Jubilee cycle is built on the sabbatical framework: just as the week leads to the Sabbath, the cycle of seven-year units leads to the Jubilee. This is the same arithmetic that structures the Feast of Weeks (Shavuot, Lev 23:15-16: count 49 days to the 50th) and the Day of Atonement (Lev 25:9: blast on the tenth day of the seventh month). YHWH’s time is organized in sevens at every scale.
25.9: The ram’s horn (shofar teru'ah) is sounded throughout the land on the Day of Atonement in the 49th/50th year. The shofar blast is the sound of YHWH’s proclamation — it was blown at Sinai (Ex 19:16, 19), will announce the Day of YHWH (Joel 2:1; Amos 3:6), and calls assemblies (Num 10:3-7). Its use here ties the Jubilee proclamation to the Day of Atonement: the social and economic reset is grounded in the ritual of forgiveness and cleansing. The Jubilee is not merely an economic instrument; it is a covenant renewal.
25.10: “You shall consecrate the fiftieth year and proclaim liberty (deror) throughout the land to all its inhabitants.” The Akkadian cognate andurārum (also durārum, durāru) appears in royal edicts from Mesopotamia as early as the 22nd century BCE (Ur III period) and throughout the Old Babylonian period (18th c. BCE). These edicts canceled debts, freed debt-slaves, and restored alienated property — proclaimed by the king at the start of his reign or during crises. Mari, Nuzi, and Nippur tablets document specific andurārum declarations. Israel’s Jubilee institutionalizes the andurārum as a fixed cycle, replacing royal prerogative with divine calendar.
25.11: The Jubilee year is itself a sabbatical year — no sowing, no harvesting of aftergrowth, no grape-gathering from unpruned vines (same as Lev 25:4-5). This means years 49 and 50 (or 48-49 and 50 in alternative readings) involve consecutive fallow years, requiring significant food reserves. YHWH explicitly addresses this concern in vv.18-22 with the promise of a triple harvest in year 48 (or 6 in the sabbatical cycle). The agricultural stress is designed to create dependence on YHWH’s provision.
25.12: The Jubilee is “holy” (qodesh). Holiness language applied to time is a distinctly Israelite theological move: the Sabbath is made holy (Gen 2:3), appointed feasts are called “holy convocations” (Lev 23), and now a whole year is declared holy. The consecration of time as sacred, not just consecration of space (temple) or persons (priests), reflects Israel’s theology that YHWH’s sovereignty pervades the temporal order.
25.13: “In this year of Jubilee each of you shall return to his property.” The return to ancestral land (ahuzzah) is the central economic mechanism of the Jubilee. The tribal land allotments of the conquest era (Josh 13-21) are treated as permanent family heritage, inalienable in perpetuity. Nuzi tablets (15th-14th c. BCE Hurrian city near modern Kirkuk) document a legal fiction called tuppi marūti (adoption tablets): families sold land by legally “adopting” the buyer, circumventing inheritance-only land transfer laws. Israel’s Jubilee eliminates the need for such fictions by guaranteeing automatic return.
25.14: “When you sell to your neighbor or buy from your neighbor, you shall not wrong one another.” The prohibition of wronging ('al tonu, from yanah, “to oppress, wrong”) appears twice in this passage (vv.14, 17) — framing the economic provisions between ethical commands. ANE commercial law was primarily concerned with procedure (proper witnesses, valid contracts, sealed tablets). Israel’s law adds a moral dimension: even technically legal transactions can be wrongful if they exploit a neighbor’s desperation.
25.15: “According to the number of years after the Jubilee, you shall buy from your neighbor.” The pricing formula is essentially a lease: what is “sold” is the crop-yield for the remaining years until Jubilee, not the land itself. A parcel sold ten years after Jubilee (40 years remaining) costs more than a parcel sold 40 years after Jubilee (10 years remaining). This commodifies time rather than land, making the Jubilee framework an annual price adjustment mechanism. Ancient land transactions from Egypt and Babylon rarely included such temporal pricing logic.
25.16: “If the years are many, you shall increase the price; if the years are few, you shall reduce the price.” The inverse relationship between years remaining and price follows logically from the Jubilee lease model. The economic sophistication here is notable: this is present-value pricing of future crop yields, a concept not systematically developed until medieval and early modern European commercial law. The Jubilee system prevents both buyer fraud (paying pennies for a near-Jubilee parcel as if it were permanent) and seller fraud (asking full price for an imminent Jubilee parcel).
25.17: “You shall not wrong one another, but you shall fear your God, for I am YHWH your God.” The divine name as sanction (ani YHWH eloheikhem) grounds the economic ethics in covenant theology. This formula — “I am YHWH your God” — appears throughout the Holiness Code (Lev 18:2, 4, 30; 19:3, 4, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 25, 31, 34, 36, 37; 20:7, 24) as a motivating refrain. Unlike Hammurabi, whose code is backed by royal authority and divine patronage, Israel’s law is directly the speech of YHWH himself. The ethical demand flows from the relationship established at Exodus.
25.18: “Therefore you shall do my statutes and keep my rules and perform them, and then you will dwell in the land securely.” Security in the land (la-vetach, “in safety/security”) is a covenant promise tied to covenant obedience throughout Deuteronomy (Deut 28:1-14; 33:12). This is not merely agricultural prediction — it is covenant theology: obedience to YHWH’s economic laws, including the land-sabbath, is the condition for the land itself remaining productive and habitable. The land is presented as responsive to Israel’s covenant faithfulness (cf. Lev 26:34-35, where the land “enjoys its sabbaths” during exile).
25.19: “The land will yield its fruit, and you will eat your fill and dwell in it securely.” The agricultural fertility promised here echoes the Deuteronomic blessings (Deut 28:3-5, 8, 11-12) and the Holiness Code blessing in Lev 26:3-10. Fertility as covenant reward and infertility as covenant curse is a theological-agricultural framework attested in other ANE texts: the Ugaritic Baal cycle ties the rain-god’s activity to cosmic and agricultural fruitfulness. Israel rejects Baal as rain-giver and insists that YHWH alone controls rainfall and harvest (Deut 11:13-14).
25.20: “And if you say, ‘What shall we eat in the seventh year, if we may not sow or gather in our crop?’” The anticipated objection is practical and agricultural: how does a farming community survive a year with no planting? This rhetorical question-and-answer structure (ki tomar... ve-tziviti) is used several times in the Pentateuch to anticipate and address community concerns (cf. Ex 13:14; Deut 6:20; 7:17; 8:17). The anxiety is real: a bad sixth-year harvest followed by a seventh-year fallow could mean starvation.
25.21: “I will command my blessing on you in the sixth year, so that it will produce a crop sufficient for three years.” The threefold sixth-year harvest (enough for year 6, year 7-sabbath, and year 8-planting-through-harvest) mirrors the double-portion manna on the sixth day (Ex 16:22-26). The wilderness manna-sabbath provided a paradigm: YHWH can be trusted to provide extra before the day of rest. The Jubilee adds a second consecutive fallow year, making the provision even more extraordinary: a sixth-year crop that lasts three years would require roughly triple the normal yield.
25.22: “When you sow in the eighth year, you will be eating some of the old crop; until the ninth year... you shall eat the old.” The extended provision narrative (sixth-year harvest lasting through eighth-year planting season) provides a complete agricultural accounting. The specificity — naming the eighth and ninth years — grounds the miracle in a realistic planting-harvest timeline. Grain harvested in spring of year 6 must sustain the community through spring of year 8, an 18-24 month supply. YHWH’s provision claim is concrete and verifiable.
25.23: “The land shall not be sold in perpetuity, for the land is mine (ki li ha-aretz). For you are strangers and sojourners (gerim vetoshavim) with me.” YHWH’s ownership of the land is the theological foundation for the entire Jubilee system. In ANE property law, land could be permanently alienated (sold in perpetuity) — Mesopotamian tablets document permanent land sales going back to the Uruk period (4th millennium BCE). Egypt held royal (Pharaoh’s) ownership of all land in theory, with farmers as tenants of the crown. Israel’s framework is distinct: YHWH is the divine landlord and the people are his resident tenants (gerim vetoshavim), the same legal status Israel held in Egypt.
25.24: “Throughout all the land of your possession, you shall allow redemption (ge'ullah) for the land.” The right of redemption (go'el, “kinsman-redeemer”) is a family and clan institution: the nearest kinsman has the right and obligation to buy back alienated family property. Nuzi tablets (15th c. BCE) and Old Babylonian documents show similar family land-protection customs, where the extended family had rights of first refusal or buyback. Israel’s go'el institution extends this to a structural principle embedded in the covenant legal code.
25.25: “If your brother becomes poor and sells part of his property, then his nearest redeemer (go'alo ha-qarov) shall come and redeem what his brother has sold.” The go'el (from ga'al, to buy back, redeem, reclaim) is the nearest kinsman with means. The term connotes both the legal right and the relational obligation — a go'el who refuses to act is shamed (cf. Ruth 4:1-12, where one go'el declines and removes his sandal in public). The institution of kinship-redemption binds together economic support, land preservation, and family honor in a single legal concept.
25.26: “If a man has no one to redeem it, and he himself becomes prosperous and finds sufficient means to redeem it.” The self-redemption provision shows the flexibility of the system: kinsman-redemption is the first option, but self-redemption when resources allow is also valid. The calculation method (years remaining × annual crop yield) is the same as the sale price calculation (v.15-16). The Jubilee system thus has three release mechanisms: kinsman redemption, self-redemption, and automatic Jubilee return — creating a fail-safe network against permanent land alienation.
25.27: “He shall calculate the years since he sold it and pay back the overpayment to the man to whom he sold it, and he shall return to his property.” The refund calculation requires subtracting the years of benefit already received from the original sale price. This assumes a fixed annual land-yield value — likely the average grain harvest income per unit of land. Mesopotamian documents from Nippur and Ur record similar agricultural productivity calculations for land lease agreements, showing that standardized yield estimates were a practical tool in ANE commerce.
25.28: “But if he does not have sufficient means to recover it, then what he sold shall remain in the hand of the buyer until the year of Jubilee. In the Jubilee it shall be released, and he shall return to his property.” The Jubilee as the final backstop for all land alienation ensures that no family can be permanently dispossessed regardless of their poverty or lack of kinsmen. This makes the Jubilee a structural anti-poverty mechanism: unlike Babylonian andurārum edicts (which were occasional royal decrees), Israel’s Jubilee is calendrically inevitable, removing it from the domain of political discretion.
25.29: “If a man sells a dwelling house in a walled city, he may redeem it within a year of its sale.” Urban houses in walled cities ('ir mivctzar) operate under different rules than rural land: a one-year redemption window replaces the Jubilee mechanism. The distinction reflects different economic realities: urban real estate value is not dependent on agricultural productivity cycles. The one-year window likely reflects standard commercial practice for house transactions in ANE cities — Mesopotamian house-sale tablets commonly specify conditions and timelines for transaction reversal.
25.30: “If it is not redeemed within a full year, then the house in the walled city shall belong permanently to the buyer... it shall not go out in the Jubilee.” Urban houses are treated as permanent property, unlike rural land. This exception acknowledges that city development — walls, infrastructure, close-packed housing — creates a different kind of property that cannot simply revert to tribal agricultural heritage. It also recognizes that Levites and city-dwellers may not have tribal agricultural allotments. The urban/rural distinction in property law is ancient; it appears already in Sumerian city-state records (3rd millennium BCE).
25.31: Houses in villages without walls (chatzerot, “courts/unwalled settlements”) are treated as rural land — subject to Jubilee return. The walled/unwalled distinction maps roughly onto permanent settlement vs. agricultural hamlet. Archaeologically, Late Bronze Age Canaan had a mix of fortified city-states (Hazor, Megiddo, Gezer) and surrounding agricultural villages with no walls. This legal distinction aligns with that social geography.
25.32: “As for the cities of the Levites, the houses in the cities of their possession — the Levites may redeem at any time.” The Levites received no tribal land allotment (Num 18:20-24; Josh 13:14) — their inheritance is YHWH himself, supported by tithes. They were given 48 cities with surrounding pasture lands (Num 35:1-8; Josh 21). Since these cities are their only property, the Levitical city houses are always redeemable and always return at Jubilee — unlike standard urban houses. This exception protects the Levites from permanent dispossession given their unique economic situation.
25.33: “And if one of the Levites does not exercise his right of redemption, then the house that was sold in a city they possess shall be released in the Jubilee.” The consistent Jubilee protection for Levitical property ensures that the tribe responsible for the sanctuary service always has a stable community base. The Levitical cities were distributed throughout the tribal territories (Josh 21), giving the Levites a presence in every region — a deliberate dispersal policy for the priestly-teaching class.
25.34: “But the open land around their cities may not be sold, for that is their permanent possession.” The migrash (pasture land around Levitical cities, Num 35:2-5) is absolutely inalienable — not even subject to redemption or Jubilee return because it can never be sold in the first place. The Levites’ economic survival depended on this pasture land for their livestock (a major source of tithes and sacrificial animals). Making it completely inalienable protects the institutional infrastructure of Israelite religion.
25.35: “If your brother becomes poor and cannot maintain himself with you, you shall support him as though he were a stranger and a sojourner (ger vetoshav), and he shall live with you.” The support obligation for a destitute kinsman uses the language of hospitality extended to resident foreigners: Israel is to treat their impoverished brothers as they would treat protected aliens. This inverts the social hierarchy — the foreign sojourner’s protection becomes the model for kinship obligation. The command to let him “live with you” (vechai 'immakh) is the positive counterpart to the prohibitions that follow.
25.36: “Take no interest from him or profit, but fear your God, that your brother may live beside you.” Interest-bearing loans (neshekh on money, tarbith/marbith on food) to fellow Israelites are absolutely prohibited. In contrast, Mesopotamian commercial life ran on credit. Old Babylonian merchants (tamkārum) routinely charged 20-33% annual interest on silver loans; grain loans ran at 33%. The Middle Assyrian Laws and Hittite Laws also regulated but did not prohibit interest. The Deuteronomic code (Deut 23:19-20) allows charging interest to foreigners while prohibiting it for Israelites — a distinction reflecting ANE commercial realities while protecting the covenant community.
25.37: “You shall not lend him your money at interest, nor give him your food for profit.” The two categories — money at interest (neshekh keseph) and food for profit (be-tarbith okhel) — cover the full range of loan types documented in Mesopotamian texts: silver loans (standard commercial credit) and grain loans (agricultural credit for peasant farmers). The same two categories appear in Ezek 18:8, 13, 17 and Ezek 22:12 as markers of injustice. The rabbinic expansion of these laws (ribbith law) eventually prohibited all forms of financial gain from loans to fellow Jews.
25.38: “I am YHWH your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to give you the land of Canaan, and to be your God.” The Exodus is the warrant for the interest prohibition: YHWH delivered Israel from economic exploitation in Egypt and therefore Israel must not exploit fellow Israelites with debt-interest. This covenant-historical grounding recurs throughout Leviticus and Deuteronomy: the memory of Egyptian bondage creates an ethical obligation toward the vulnerable. The same logic appears in Ex 22:21-27, Lev 19:34, and Deut 24:17-22.
25.39: “If your brother becomes poor beside you and sells himself to you, you shall not make him serve as a slave (eved): he shall be with you as a hired worker (sakir) and as a sojourner (toshav).” The debt-slave is redefined as a hired laborer with sojourner status — not chattel property. This is a significant legal category shift: the eved (slave) has no legal standing; the sakir (hired hand) has contractual rights. In Babylon, debt-slaves were legally property and could be sold, assigned as collateral, and inherited. Israel’s law denies this commodification for Israelite persons.
25.40: “He shall serve with you until the year of Jubilee. Then he shall go out from you, he and his children with him.” Jubilee release applies to Israelite debt-slaves (and their families). This differs from the septennial release in Ex 21:2-6 and Deut 15:12-18, which releases a Hebrew slave after 6 years. Scholars debate whether these are parallel institutions or successive refinements. The Jubilee release has the advantage of being calendrically fixed and community-wide rather than individually calculated.
25.41: “And he shall go back to his own family and return to the possession of his fathers.” Release from debt-slavery includes restoration to family and land. The double restoration (family + land) is the full expression of the Jubilee’s social vision: not merely freedom from bondage, but reintegration into the social network (family) and economic network (land) that enables full participation in Israelite community life. Babylonian andurārum edicts freed debt-slaves but did not typically include land restoration for the landless.
25.42: “For they are my servants (avadai), whom I brought out of the land of Egypt; they shall not be sold as slaves.” YHWH’s ownership claim (same language as the Decalogue prologue, “I am YHWH your God who brought you out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery”) is the final theological ground for the anti-slavery provisions. Israelites cannot be permanent slaves to other humans because they already belong to YHWH. The Exodus defined their status: former slaves of Pharaoh, now servants of YHWH. A servant of YHWH cannot simultaneously be a slave to another.
25.43: “You shall not rule over him ruthlessly (be-farekhk), but shall fear your God.” Parekhk (H6531) = crushing, ruthless labor — the identical term used for Egypt’s treatment of Israel in Ex 1:13-14 (“the Egyptians ruthlessly made the people of Israel work as slaves... making their lives bitter with hard service”). The prohibition against parekhk creates a direct legal link between the Exodus memory and Israelite labor law: do not be what Egypt was to you. This motivation — the slave-turned-master must not replicate the oppressor — is a distinctive feature of Israelite social ethics.
25.44: “As for your male and female slaves whom you may have: you may buy male and female slaves from among the nations that are around you.” Foreign slaves could be owned permanently. This provision is jarring by modern standards and has been extensively discussed in the history of interpretation. In ANE context, permanent servitude of war captives and purchased foreigners was universal — Hammurabi’s Code, Egyptian records, Hittite laws, and Ugaritic texts all document chattel slavery of foreigners. Israel’s law reduces but does not eliminate the institution, carving out extensive protections for Israelite persons while accepting the surrounding social reality for foreigners.
25.45: “You may also buy from among the strangers who sojourn with you and their clans that are with you, who have been born in your land.” Resident aliens (gerim) and their locally-born descendants could also be bought as slaves. This creates a legal distinction between ger (protected sojourner with many rights) and ger-as-slave: the sojourner is protected in most law, but can become permanently enslaved in economic distress. The Holiness Code balances extensive protections for the sojourner (Lev 19:33-34; 24:22) with this exception for the slave trade.
25.46: “You may bequeath them to your sons after you to inherit as a possession forever.” Foreign slaves could be inherited as property — the same legal category as land or livestock. Old Babylonian inheritance records list slaves among estates, with names, prices, and legal descriptions. The permanent vs. temporary servitude distinction (foreigners permanent, Israelites temporary) reflects the covenant-community boundary: YHWH’s special ownership claim applies to Israel, while the general ANE social institution of slavery applies to non-Israelites within Israel’s jurisdiction.
25.47: “If a stranger or sojourner with you becomes rich, and your brother beside him becomes poor and sells himself to the stranger or sojourner.” The scenario of an Israelite entering debt-slavery to a wealthy resident alien is the most legally complex case addressed. In it, the normal social hierarchy (Israelite > sojourner) is economically reversed. ANE documents from Nuzi and Nippur show that resident merchants could become wealthier than local landowners and that debt relationships crossed ethnic lines. This case addresses the pastoral reality that economic reversal happened in Israel too.
25.48: “After he is sold he may be redeemed. One of his brothers may redeem him.” Even when sold to a foreigner, the kinsman-redeemer (go'el) has the right to buy the Israelite back. The go'el institution becomes a counterforce to permanent foreign ownership of Israelite persons. The same go'el principle underlies Ruth 4 (Boaz as redeemer of Naomi’s land and Ruth), Jeremiah 32 (Jeremiah purchases ancestral land as a sign-act of restoration), and the theological use of ga'al for YHWH’s redemption of Israel from Egypt (Ex 6:6; 15:13).
25.49: “Or his uncle or his uncle’s son may redeem him, or a close relative from his clan may redeem him. Or if he grows rich he may redeem himself.” The hierarchy of redeemers (brother, uncle, cousin, clansman, self) establishes concentric circles of responsibility. This kinship-solidarity structure is documented in other ANE cultures: the Nuzi tuppi ahhutti (brotherhood tablets) established formal kinship relationships with mutual support obligations. Israel’s go'el institution regularizes an existing ANE kinship-solidarity custom and embeds it in covenant law.
25.50: “He shall calculate with his buyer from the year when he sold himself to him until the year of Jubilee, and the price of his sale shall be according to the number of years.” The redemption calculation is based on remaining years of service — the same formula as land redemption (vv.15-16). This treats the person’s labor as having a time-based value: total service ÷ total years × price paid. The computation requires agreed-upon annual labor rates, which ANE administrative documents (especially Mesopotamian ration lists and labor contracts) show were standardized by type of work and skill level.
25.51: “If there are still many years left, he shall pay proportionately for his redemption some of his sale price.” The proportional redemption formula is equitable to both parties: the buyer gets reimbursed for years of service not yet rendered; the slave is not penalized for being redeemed early. This contrasts with some ANE redemption practices where a flat fee (not proportional) was required for early manumission. The proportional system assumes that the initial sale price reflected expected labor value, and the return of unused value is the basis for the buyback price.
25.52: “If there remain but a few years until the year of Jubilee, he shall calculate and pay for his redemption in proportion to his years of service.” The formula works symmetrically in both directions: large buyback price (many years remaining) or small buyback price (few years remaining). A slave sold one year before Jubilee would cost nearly nothing to redeem; one sold the year after Jubilee would cost close to the original sale price. This creates a natural economic pressure against selling Israelites into debt-slavery in the years immediately after Jubilee (when the service period is longest and most valuable).
25.53: “He shall treat him as a worker hired year by year. He shall not rule over him ruthlessly (be-farekhk) in your sight.” The parekhk prohibition appears again (cf. v.43), this time addressed to the community: “in your sight” — the community has an obligation to observe and prevent ruthless treatment. This is a collective responsibility norm. ANE slavery law rarely imposed community oversight obligations on slave treatment; Israel’s law creates social accountability for how Israelite debt-slaves are treated even by foreign owners.
25.54: “And if he is not redeemed by these means, then he and his children shall be released in the year of Jubilee.” The Jubilee is the absolute backstop: even without a redeemer and without self-redemption, freedom comes automatically in the Jubilee year. No ANE institution provides an equivalent guaranteed, calendrically fixed, unconditional release. The Babylonian andurārum depended on a new king’s political will; the Hittite debt-release edicts were irregular. Israel’s Jubilee is unique precisely in its systematic, YHWH-guaranteed character.
25.55: “For it is to me that the people of Israel are servants (avadim). They are my servants whom I brought out of the land of Egypt: I am YHWH your God.” The chapter closes by returning to the foundational claim: Israel’s servants because they are YHWH’s servants first. The divine name (ani YHWH eloheikhem) ends the chapter as it has punctuated it throughout, grounding the entire social and economic legislation in the covenant identity established at the Exodus. The land is mine (v.23); the people are mine (v.42, 55) — YHWH’s ownership is the axis around which the Jubilee revolves.
